proposals.title: Interstitial Site Occupation of Co2+ and Ni2+ Dopants in 2D Hybrid Perovskites: A Structural and Transport Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work on 2D hybrid perovskites has established that transition metal doping is a powerful strategy for tuning electronic and thermal transport properties, crucial for thermoelectric and optoelectronic applications. Recent studies on Mn2+ doping in (PEA)2PbI4 (PEA = phenethylammonium) have revealed unexpected interstitial site occupancy, which dramatically alters local structure and charge transport. Systematically testing whether this is a general phenomenon for other 3d transition metals is essential to establish design rules for defect engineering in these soft, complex lattices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Co2+ and Ni2+ ions, due to their distinct ionic radii, crystal field stabilization energies, and preferred coordination geometries compared to Mn2+, will also occupy interstitial sites in 2D perovskite lattices, but that the specific site and its local distortion will differ, leading to unique impacts on band structure and thermal transport.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of single crystals of (PEA)2Pb1-xMxI4 (M = Co2+, Ni2+) using slow cooling methods. Their crystal structures will be solved using single-crystal X-ray diffraction, with particular focus on identifying interstitial sites via difference Fourier maps and refining occupancy factors. Electronic structure will be probed via density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the VASP code to model defect formation energies and band structure changes. Thermal and electrical transport properties (Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity) will be measured on oriented single crystals to correlate structural findings with thermoelectric performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a broader understanding of defect chemistry in 2D perovskites, moving beyond isolated examples to a comparative study of 3d ions. It will directly link specific dopant-induced structural modulations—akin to the incommensurate modulations studied in chalcogenides by Kanatzidis—to changes in transport, providing a materials design map for targeted property enhancement.
proposals.novelty: While Mn2+ interstitial doping has been observed, it is non-obvious whether smaller, higher-charge-density ions like Co2+ and Ni2+ will exhibit similar behavior, or if they will preferentially substitute for Pb2+, given their different chemical hard-soft characteristics and potential for Jahn-Teller effects.

proposals.title: Single-Atom Dopant Control of Electronic and Thermal Transport: Probing a Single Mn2+ Interstitial in a Chalcogenide Host via Optically Detected Magnetic Resonance
proposals.motivation: The performance of thermoelectric and electronic materials is critically governed by point defects, yet ensemble measurements average over heterogeneous defect populations, obscuring the fundamental contribution of individual dopants. Pre-2026, the Kanatzidis group has mastered the synthesis of complex chalcogenides with tunable structures and off-centering atoms, but the atomistic role of a single interstitial ion on local band structure and phonon scattering remains unexplored. Directly probing a single paramagnetic dopant, such as Mn2+, would bridge the gap between macroscopic transport properties and atomic-scale defect physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single interstitial Mn2+ ion in a diamagnetic chalcogenide host (e.g., AgInSe2 or a related diamondoid compound) creates a highly localized perturbation to the conduction band and generates a distinct strain field, which can be quantitatively correlated with its spin resonance signature and local electronic environment.
proposals.method: We will synthesize high-quality single crystals of candidate hosts (e.g., AgInSe2) with ultra-dilute Mn2+ doping (<0.01 at.%) using established molten-flux or vapor transport techniques. Individual Mn2+ ions (S=5/2) will be isolated and probed using a cryogenic confocal microscope integrated with a microwave resonator for optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR). Specific pulse sequences, including Hahn echo and dynamical decoupling, will be employed to measure the spin Hamiltonian parameters (zero-field splitting, hyperfine coupling) and coherence times (T1, T2), which report on the local crystal field and charge distribution. Concurrent scanning probe microscopy (AFM/KPFM) on the same crystal will map the local electrostatic potential perturbation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct experimental protocol for correlating the quantum state of a single dopant with its local electronic and structural impact in a functional material. It will provide the first microscopic validation of defect-driven band engineering and phonon scattering models central to thermoelectrics. The findings will create a new paradigm for 'defect spectroscopy' in materials chemistry, moving beyond ensemble averages.
proposals.novelty: While single-spin resonance is mature in quantum information science, its application to isolate and study a *deliberately introduced* dopant atom within a complex, high-performance thermoelectric material is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond studying intrinsic defects in wide-bandgap semiconductors to actively engineer and probe single dopants in narrow-gap, high-mobility compounds.

proposals.title: Gradient n-Type Structures in Diamondoid Chalcogenides: A New Paradigm for Band Engineering and Thermoelectric Transport
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 thermoelectric research, particularly on diamondoid compounds like AgInSe2, emphasizes the need for precise control of carrier concentration (n) to optimize the power factor. However, the synthesis of phase-pure, homogeneous single crystals with a single optimal n-value is a stringent and often limiting requirement. This proposal challenges that paradigm by exploring intentionally engineered, spatially graded n-value structures, which could create internal electric fields and novel transport regimes not accessible in homogeneous materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intentionally fabricating gradient n-type structures within a single crystal or oriented polycrystalline matrix of a diamondoid chalcogenide (e.g., AgInSe2) will create a built-in potential that beneficially scatters low-energy carriers while maintaining high mobility for high-energy carriers, thereby enhancing the thermoelectric power factor beyond the limits of homogeneous samples.
proposals.method: We will synthesize gradient structures using a controlled vapor transport method with a dynamic source composition, gradually varying the dopant (e.g., halogen for Se-site) concentration along the growth axis. The resulting ingots will be characterized by high-resolution X-ray diffraction and electron probe microanalysis to map the structural and compositional gradients. Temperature-dependent Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, and Hall effect measurements will be performed with micro-probe contacts along the gradient to map the local transport properties. The data will be analyzed using a modified Boltzmann transport model that incorporates the measured compositional gradient as a spatially varying potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials design strategy that moves beyond the pursuit of homogeneous phase purity. It will provide fundamental insights into how graded doping profiles influence charge transport and scattering in complex semiconductors, potentially leading to a new class of 'functionally graded' thermoelectric materials with enhanced performance.
proposals.novelty: While compositional gradients are studied in heterostructures, applying this concept intentionally within a single-phase, single-crystal-like diamondoid system to manipulate thermoelectric transport is a novel and non-obvious extension of Kanatzidis's work on intrinsic off-centering and band structure engineering.

proposals.title: High-Performance Near-Infrared Photodetectors from Phase-Pure, n-Type Diamondoid Chalcogenide Single Crystals
proposals.motivation: The development of high-performance, low-cost infrared optoelectronic materials is crucial for applications in sensing, imaging, and telecommunications. While Kanatzidis's group has demonstrated exceptional thermoelectric performance in n-type diamondoid compounds like AgInSe2, the optoelectronic potential of these high-quality, phase-pure single crystals remains largely unexplored. Translating superior bulk electronic transport properties into functional devices would bridge a significant gap between materials discovery and practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high electron mobility, favorable band structure, and low defect density of phase-pure n-type diamondoid chalcogenide single crystals (e.g., AgInSe2 and its homologues) will enable efficient charge carrier extraction, leading to photodetectors with high responsivity and fast response times in the near-infrared region.
proposals.method: We will fabricate lateral photoconductor devices using exfoliated flakes or precisely cleaved single crystals of target materials (e.g., AgInSe2, and related A-M-Q phases). Metal contacts (Au, Ti/Au) will be deposited via electron-beam lithography and thermal evaporation. Device performance will be characterized by measuring current-voltage characteristics in dark and under illumination from tunable lasers (covering visible to ~1.5 μm). Key figures of merit—responsivity, external quantum efficiency, specific detectivity (D*), and response speed—will be extracted. Temperature-dependent photoconductivity and impedance spectroscopy will be used to elucidate carrier recombination mechanisms and interface properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative link between the exceptional bulk transport properties of these novel diamondoid semiconductors and their thin-crystal device performance, providing a benchmark for infrared-active chalcogenides. It will deliver a new materials platform for infrared optoelectronics and create a roadmap for integrating complex, high-performance thermoelectric crystals into functional photonic devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly leveraging the meticulously optimized *electronic* transport properties, achieved through prior band and crystal structure engineering for thermoelectrics, for a fundamentally different application in optoelectronics, a crossover not previously demonstrated for this materials family.

proposals.title: Mapping Local Structural Ensembles in Complex Chalcogenides: Linking Off-Center Distortions to Carrier Transport and Luminescence
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work on complex materials like AgInSe2 and incommensurate A-Ta-Se phases reveals that average crystallographic structure often fails to explain key optoelectronic and thermoelectric properties. The field lacks a mechanistic understanding of how specific, local lattice distortions—such as the intrinsic off-centering of Ag+ ions—directly govern electronic band structure, carrier scattering, and recombination pathways. Establishing this causative link is critical for the rational design of next-generation functional materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The populations and dynamics of specific local structural states (e.g., off-center cation configurations, modulated domain boundaries) act as a primary 'allosteric' control mechanism, directly determining discrete optoelectronic performance metrics like photoluminescence quantum yield and electronic thermal conductivity in complex chalcogenides.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single crystals of targeted systems (e.g., diamondoid AgInSe2, modulated Rb/Cs-Ta-Se). Local structural ensembles will be mapped using high-resolution techniques adapted from biophysics: cryogenic scanning transmission electron microscopy (cryo-STEM) with 4D-STEM for lattice distortion mapping, and spatially correlated photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Advanced pair distribution function (PDF) analysis from synchrotron X-ray total scattering will quantify local distortion populations. These local structural maps will be directly correlated with point-by-point measurements of carrier lifetime (via time-resolved microwave conductivity) and thermal transport.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for understanding structure-property relationships in complex materials by shifting focus from the average to the local structure. It will provide the first direct, causative maps linking specific distortion motifs to electronic and thermal transport metrics. The methodology will create a generalizable framework for diagnosing and engineering performance-limiting local structures in functional materials.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard crystallography-first approach by treating the material as a dynamic ensemble of local states, a concept borrowed from structural biology. The direct, spatially correlated mapping of local structure to local electronic function using adapted biophysical techniques represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance beyond ensemble-averaged measurements.

proposals.title: Structural Instability as a Driver of Microstructural Evolution: Probing the Causal Link Between Sn/Ge Heterogeneity and Thermal Degradation in Mixed Perovskites
proposals.motivation: Lead-free Sn-Ge perovskites are promising for optoelectronics, but their rapid degradation under thermal stress remains a critical barrier. Pre-2026 studies note Ge-rich nanoprecipitate formation, yet the fundamental structural drivers of this phase separation and its direct link to material degradation are unknown. Understanding this is essential to design stable, high-performance lead-free alternatives.
proposals.hypothesis: The inherent structural instability of the mixed Sn-Ge perovskite lattice, driven by the distinct bonding preferences and lattice dynamics of Sn(II), directly causes the nucleation and growth of Ge-rich secondary phases, which act as the primary microstructural signature and mechanism of thermal degradation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of A(Sn_xGe_{1-x})I_3 and A(Sn_xGe_{1-x})Br_3 (A=Cs, MA) single crystals. Their structural evolution under controlled thermal aging will be tracked using temperature-dependent single-crystal X-ray diffraction to monitor lattice dynamics and in-situ synchrotron powder diffraction to detect phase nucleation. The local composition and transport properties of resulting heterogeneous microstructures will be mapped using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and scanning thermal probe microscopy, correlating Ge-rich precipitate morphology with degraded thermal and electronic transport.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal mechanism linking the intrinsic structural chemistry of Sn/Ge perovskites to their operational degradation, moving beyond phenomenological observation. It will provide design rules—such as halide selection and stoichiometric limits—to suppress deleterious phase separation, advancing the rational design of stable lead-free perovskites.
proposals.novelty: This approach treats Ge-rich nanoprecipitates not as a mere symptom but as a structural probe to decode the fundamental non-lead-like instability of the Sn-Ge lattice, a perspective absent from prior analogies with Pb-Sn systems. It directly connects lattice-level bonding preferences to macroscopic thermal transport degradation.

proposals.title: Divergent Structural and Electronic Evolution in Halide-Substituted Pb vs. Sn Hybrid Perovskites: A Crystallographic and Transport Study
proposals.motivation: Lead-halide perovskites are high-performance optoelectronic and thermoelectric materials, but their toxicity drives the search for Sn-based analogues. Pre-2026 work often assumes Pb and Sn perovskites are structurally analogous under halide substitution (I, Br, Cl), guiding property predictions. This assumption overlooks the distinct chemical preferences of Pb2+ and Sn2+ (e.g., lone pair stereochemical activity, orbital hybridization), which could lead to critical differences in structure-property relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Pb and Sn hybrid perovskites undergo fundamentally different structural distortions and electronic band structure evolution upon halide substitution, due to the contrasting influence of relativistic effects (Pb) versus stronger p-orbital coupling (Sn). This divergence will lead to non-analogous thermal and charge transport properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a homologous series of A-site cation-tuned Pb and Sn perovskites, AMX3 (M = Pb, Sn; X = I, Br, Cl), growing high-quality single crystals for definitive structure determination. Using single-crystal X-ray diffraction and pair distribution function analysis, we will quantify deviations from ideal cubic symmetry, map distortion modes, and identify potential incommensurate modulations. Electronic structure will be probed via temperature-dependent Hall effect and Seebeck coefficient measurements, complemented by DFT band structure calculations focusing on band degeneracy and effective mass near the Fermi level for thermoelectric assessment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete, atomistic understanding of how cation identity dictates the structural and electronic response to chemical pressure in halide perovskites. It will provide design rules for Sn-based materials that move beyond simple Pb-mimicry and could reveal new, high-performance compositions with optimized thermal transport for thermoelectrics or tailored band gaps for photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While structural studies of individual Pb or Sn perovskites exist, a systematic, side-by-side crystallographic and transport investigation challenging the assumption of their analogous behavior is lacking. The proposal leverages Kanatzidis's expertise in complex chalcogenide structure-property relationships and applies it to the halide perovskite field, focusing on homologous series and intrinsic off-centering effects.

proposals.title: Mapping Compositional and Strain-Induced Electronic Topology Transitions in Complex Chalcogenides for Anomalous Thermoelectric Response
proposals.motivation: Non-monotonic property evolution in alloy systems, such as the sharp peaks in thermoelectric figure of merit (zT), often lack a predictive microscopic model. Pre-2026 work, including Kanatzidis's studies on diamondoid AgInSe2 and incommensurate A-Ta-Se phases, highlights complex structural-electronic interplay but lacks a systematic framework to detect electronic phase transitions that occur without symmetry change, like Lifshitz transitions or band inversion shifts, which can drastically alter transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anomalous, non-monotonic evolution of electronic and thermal transport properties in complex chalcogenide alloys and modulated structures is governed by a hidden landscape of electronic topological transitions (ETTs) that can be systematically mapped as a function of composition and strain.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated high-throughput DFT workflow using the AFLOW and pymatgen ecosystems to scan composition/strain phase spaces in target systems (e.g., AgInSe2-alloys, A-Ta-Se homologues). The workflow will integrate phonon stability calculations (via Phonopy) with precise electronic structure analysis to compute band curvature, Fermi surface topology, and Z2 invariants. Machine learning models (graph neural networks) will be trained on these descriptors to predict the locations of structural and electronic phase boundaries, explicitly identifying Lifshitz transitions and band inversion points.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified computational framework to predict electronic structure discontinuities alongside structural instabilities, directly linking them to property anomalies. It will deliver a public database of ETT maps for chalcogenide systems, offering a new design principle for optimizing thermoelectric and electronic materials through targeted navigation of electronic phase space.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput structural stability screening is established, the simultaneous, automated detection of electronic topological transitions—particularly those uncoupled from symmetry breaking—within a materials discovery pipeline is a novel and untapped approach to explain property discontinuities.

proposals.title: Uncovering Discontinuous Electronic Structure Evolution in Tunable Chalcogenides via Multi-Probe Spectroscopic Fingerprinting
proposals.motivation: The tuning of electronic band structure via composition, strain, or dimensionality is foundational to designing semiconductors for thermoelectrics and optoelectronics. Current models predominantly assume smooth, monotonic property changes, yet complex structural phenomena like incommensurate modulation and off-centering in chalcogenides suggest potential for hidden electronic phase transitions. Identifying such discontinuities is critical for accurate materials design and understanding performance limits.
proposals.hypothesis: The electronic structure evolution during bandgap tuning in complex chalcogenides is not universally smooth but can involve discontinuous transitions or metastable intermediate states, detectable as correlated, abrupt changes across multiple high-resolution spectroscopic signatures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of tunable chalcogenides (e.g., AgInSe2-based alloys, A-Ta-Se systems) and subject them to in-situ tuning via temperature, pressure, or composition gradients. Simultaneously, we will employ a coordinated suite of high-resolution probes: time-resolved X-ray diffraction for structural dynamics, temperature-dependent Raman spectroscopy for vibrational modes, and high-resolution UV-vis and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy for electronic structure. Data will be analyzed via multivariate correlation algorithms to identify discontinuities where spectral features change non-monotonically and in concert, signaling a hidden transition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new diagnostic framework for detecting hidden electronic phase transitions, moving beyond averaged property measurements. It will provide a refined map of structure-property relationships in tunable semiconductors, directly impacting the rational design of next-generation thermoelectric and optoelectronic materials by revealing previously overlooked stability regimes and performance cliffs.
proposals.novelty: The approach adapts the multi-technique fingerprinting strategy from protein folding to solid-state chemistry, seeking non-monotonic signatures in electronic structure evolution—a paradigm shift from the prevailing assumption of smooth tunability in semiconductor alloys.

proposals.title: Dynamic Lone Pair Stereochemistry in SnTe1-xSex: Chemical Pressure as a Direct Handle for Tuning Local Structure and Electronic Anharmonicity
proposals.motivation: The stereochemically active 5s2 lone pair in Sn2+ is a crucial design element for functional materials, driving ferroelectricity, large thermoelectric power factors, and anharmonic phonon scattering. However, its expression is typically treated as a static, binary feature. Pre-2026 work lacks a systematic study of how continuous chemical pressure, distinct from cation doping, can dynamically tune lone pair activity and its coupled electronic and structural responses in a single material system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemical pressure from anionic alloying (Te/Se substitution) in SnTe will provide a continuous, clean tuning parameter to modulate the stereochemical activity of the Sn2+ lone pair, leading to predictable, non-monotonic changes in local structure distortion, electronic band convergence near the valence band maximum, and emergent anharmonic properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize high-quality single crystals and polycrystalline samples of the SnTe1-xSex solid solution series (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) using molten flux and Bridgman methods. Local structure, including Sn off-centering and anharmonicity, will be probed via pair distribution function (PDF) analysis from synchrotron X-ray total scattering and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS). Electronic structure evolution will be calculated using density functional theory (DFT) with the modified Becke-Johnson (mBJ) potential and validated by temperature-dependent Seebeck and Hall effect measurements. Property mapping will focus on lattice thermal conductivity (via laser flash analysis), piezoelectric coefficient (d33), and second harmonic generation (SHG) intensity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, composition-structure-property map for lone pair engineering via anionic substitution, moving beyond qualitative descriptors. It will reveal how chemical pressure specifically modifies the Born effective charges and polarizability underlying piezoelectric and nonlinear optical responses in chalcogenides, providing new design rules for energy conversion and optoelectronic materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the lone pair not as a fixed entity but as a dynamically tunable degree of freedom via chemical pressure, and in directly correlating this tunability with a suite of properties (thermal, electronic, optical) typically studied in isolation, within a single, well-defined homologous series.

proposals.title: Mapping the Thermodynamic Landscape of Stereochemically Active Lone Pairs in FASnI3-xBrx via In Situ X-ray Spectroscopy and Total Scattering
proposals.motivation: The stereochemical activity of the Sn2+ 5s2 lone pair is a critical electronic structure feature governing the optoelectronic and thermoelectric properties of halide perovskites like FASnI3. However, its dynamic response to thermodynamic perturbations (temperature, pressure) remains poorly understood, limiting our ability to rationally control materials properties. This knowledge gap mirrors the challenge of mapping protein conformational landscapes, requiring analogous in situ structural probes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stereochemical expression of the Sn2+ lone pair in FASnI3-xBrx is a dynamic, tunable function of temperature and pressure, directly driving reversible antiferroelectric distortions and modulating the electronic band structure near the valence band maximum.
proposals.method: We will employ in situ variable-temperature (10-400 K) and variable-pressure (0-10 GPa) X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) at the Sn K-edge to probe the local coordination geometry and electron density. Simultaneously, high-energy X-ray total scattering with pair distribution function (PDF) analysis will quantify the real-space local and medium-range structural distortions. Data will be analyzed using established codes (e.g., FEFF for XANES, PDFgui/DiffPy-CMI for PDF modeling) to extract precise bond lengths, angles, and off-centering parameters, constructing a phase diagram of lone pair activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct, dynamic map linking thermodynamic variables to lone pair stereochemistry and lattice distortions in a halide perovskite. It will provide fundamental design principles for tuning carrier effective mass and lattice thermal conductivity via lone pair engineering, directly impacting thermoelectric and photovoltaic materials discovery.
proposals.novelty: While lone pairs are known to be important, applying the combined in situ XAS/PDF methodology—common in studying amorphous or nanocrystalline systems—to map the real-time, local structural dynamics of a lone pair under thermodynamic perturbation in these materials is novel. This approach moves beyond static, average structure determinations from conventional diffraction.

proposals.title: Pressure- and Temperature-Induced Modulation of Stereochemically Active 5s2 Lone Pairs and Antiferroelectricity in the Mixed-Halide Perovskite Series FASnI3-xBrx
proposals.motivation: The dynamic stereochemical activity of ns2 lone pairs (e.g., Sn2+ 5s2) is a critical design principle for functional materials, driving ferroelectricity, thermoelectricity, and unusual thermal transport. In hybrid perovskites like FASnI3, the lone pair drives a low-temperature antiferroelectric phase transition, but the interplay between chemical substitution (Br/I), external pressure, and lone pair expression remains unexplored. Understanding this interplay is essential for rationally controlling electronic structure and phase stability in these technologically relevant materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applied hydrostatic pressure and Br substitution will cooperatively suppress the stereochemical activity of the Sn2+ 5s2 lone pair, stabilizing the higher-symmetry cubic phase by reducing the off-centering distortion, and that this can be mapped onto a unified structural phase diagram.
proposals.method: We will synthesize high-quality single crystals of the FASnI3-xBrx series (x = 0 to 3). Variable-temperature (10-400 K) and variable-pressure (up to 5 GPa) single-crystal X-ray diffraction will be used to track the evolution of the Sn2+ off-centering, octahedral tilts, and modulation vectors. This structural data will be correlated with temperature- and pressure-dependent transport measurements (electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient) and Raman spectroscopy to link lone pair dynamics to changes in band structure and lattice dynamics, guided by density functional theory (DFT) calculations on the experimentally determined structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative structure-property phase diagram linking chemical pressure (Br substitution), physical pressure, and lone pair-driven distortions in a hybrid perovskite. It will provide fundamental insights into how to tune electronic dimensionality and carrier scattering mechanisms via lone pair engineering, with direct implications for thermoelectric and optoelectronic applications of ns2-cation materials.
proposals.novelty: While lone pair activity in Sn-based perovskites is known, a combined high-pressure and mixed-halide study to decouple chemical and physical pressure effects on the dynamic lone pair and its associated antiferroelectric order is unprecedented. The approach leverages Kanatzidis group expertise in modulated structures and transport in complex chalcogenides to a new class of hybrid materials.

proposals.title: Atomistic Mapping of Non-Radiative Pathways in Complex Chalcogenides via Femtosecond X-ray Absorption and Optical Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: In complex thermoelectric and photovoltaic chalcogenides like AgInSe2 and A-Ta-Se phases, non-radiative recombination limits efficiency, but its atomistic origin remains speculative. Pre-2026 ultrafast spectroscopy reveals carrier lifetimes, while static XAFS provides average structure, but a direct, simultaneous link between transient electronic states and specific, picosecond-scale atomic motions is missing. Establishing this causal link is critical for rationally designing materials with suppressed loss channels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dominant non-radiative pathways in diamondoid and incommensurate chalcogenides are triggered by specific, ultrafast local symmetry-breaking distortions—such as transient off-centering of Ag/In or collective Se displacements—which create a transient structural configuration that efficiently scatters charge carriers.
proposals.method: We will employ synchronized ultrafast optical pump (e.g., 800 nm, ~50 fs) and element-specific X-ray probe pulses at a femtosecond X-ray free-electron laser (e.g., LCLS). Simultaneous time-resolved X-ray absorption fine structure (tr-XAFS) at the Ag K-edge, In K-edge, and Se K-edge will track sub-Ångstrom bond length and coordination changes around each species with ~100 fs resolution. Concurrent broadband transient absorption/reflectivity will monitor electronic relaxation dynamics. Data analysis will involve fitting EXAFS spectra to extract time-dependent radial distribution functions and correlating these structural trajectories with kinetic models of carrier decay.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first causally linked, element-resolved movie of non-radiative recombination in complex chalcogenides, moving beyond correlation to mechanism. It will identify the specific atomic degrees of freedom responsible for efficiency loss, providing a foundational design rule—'structural motifs to avoid'—for next-generation energy materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous, multimodal measurement on single-crystal samples of complex materials, directly pairing electronic kinetics with the structural dynamics of multiple, distinct atomic sites, which has not been achieved pre-2026 for such structurally diverse and incommensurate systems.

proposals.title: Hierarchical GW and Embedded Wavefunction Modeling of Defect States in Lone-Pair Disordered Thermoelectrics: The Case of Diamondoid AgInSe2
proposals.motivation: Disordered materials with stereochemically active lone pairs, such as the high-performance thermoelectric AgInSe2, exhibit complex electronic structures where intrinsic off-centering creates shallow and deep trap states. These states critically control carrier concentration and mobility, yet their accurate computational prediction remains a major challenge. Standard DFT fails to describe band gaps and defect level positions quantitatively, hindering the rational design of next-generation thermoelectric and optoelectronic materials.
proposals.hypothesis: A tiered computational strategy combining large-scale DFT supercells for disorder, the GW approximation for quasiparticle band structures and shallow traps, and embedded multireference wavefunction methods for deep traps will yield quantitatively accurate predictions of electronic structure and defect energetics in lone-pair disordered semiconductors.
proposals.method: We will construct large supercells of diamondoid AgInSe2 to model intrinsic off-centering disorder using DFT (SCAN meta-GGA). Candidate defect configurations will be identified from these models. The quasiparticle band structure and shallow trap levels will be computed using the one-shot G0W0 method on top of DFT. For deep, localized trap states, we will employ embedded cluster models, where the local region is treated with high-level multireference methods (e.g., CASSCF/NEVPT2) and embedded in a periodic DFT potential using quantum embedding techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first definitive, first-principles map of trap states in a high-performance lone-pair thermoelectric, directly linking atomic-scale disorder to electronic transport properties. It will establish a validated hierarchical computational protocol for disordered materials, moving beyond qualitative DFT descriptions to quantitative accuracy comparable to experiment.
proposals.novelty: The integration of GW corrections for shallow states with embedded multireference wavefunction methods for deep traps in a single workflow for a disordered material is a novel, non-trivial advance beyond standard defect calculations. It directly addresses the dual challenge of extended band structure and strongly correlated localized states inherent to these systems.

proposals.title: Time-Resolved Mapping of Lone-Pair Dynamics and Non-Radiative Losses in Chalcogenide Thermoelectrics via Ultrafast Spectroscopy and Ab Initio Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In complex chalcogenides like AgInSe2 and A-Ta-Se phases, lone-pair-induced structural distortions and dynamic disorder are critical for low thermal conductivity but also create non-radiative recombination centers that limit optoelectronic efficiency. Pre-2026, the real-time atomic pathways of these detrimental distortions and their direct link to carrier lifetime remain poorly understood, hindering the rational design of materials that decouple beneficial phonon scattering from electronic losses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic evolution of lone-pair-induced off-centering, probed by ultrafast spectroscopy, can be directly correlated with first-principles simulations of electron-phonon coupling to identify and quantify specific atomic motions responsible for non-radiative recombination.
proposals.method: We will employ ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) and frozen phonon calculations within DFT to model the potential energy surfaces and time-dependent structural evolution in target systems like AgInSe2. These simulations will guide the interpretation of ultrafast electron diffraction (UED) and transient absorption spectroscopy experiments, which provide direct, femtosecond-scale probes of local structure and carrier dynamics. The experimental data will serve as a benchmark for refining the theoretical models, particularly the strength of electron-phonon coupling derived from density functional perturbation theory (DFPT).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated, dynamic model linking specific structural motifs to non-radiative pathways in complex chalcogenides. It will provide a new design principle for engineering materials where dynamic disorder is preserved for thermoelectric performance but its electronic detrimental effects are mitigated, advancing the development of high-efficiency energy conversion materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, iterative feedback between time-resolved structural probes and first-principles dynamics to deconvolute complex structural evolution, moving beyond static crystal structure analysis to engineer materials based on the control of transient atomic trajectories.

proposals.title: Decoding Electronic Structure Evolution in Incommensurate and Homologous Chalcogenides: The Role of Lone Pairs, Disorder, and Band Convergence
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the Kanatzidis group has discovered complex material families, like the incommensurate A14+2/3Ta8Se46+2/3 and stoichiometrically conserved homologous series, which exhibit rich structural diversity and promising thermoelectric properties. The electronic origins of their transport behavior, particularly the role of stereochemically active lone pairs (e.g., from Sn2+, Pb2+, Bi3+) and inherent structural disorder, remain poorly understood, limiting rational design. Advanced DFT modeling is now critical to connect their intricate crystal structures, revealed by single-crystal studies, to measurable thermal and electronic transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interplay of stereochemically active lone pairs and incommensurate/disordered cation substructures drives a unique electronic structure evolution across these series, leading to band convergence, resonant states, or trap states that critically enhance the thermoelectric power factor and reduce lattice thermal conductivity.
proposals.method: We will perform high-throughput DFT calculations using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) with the SCAN meta-GGA functional, which better describes lone-pair effects and van der Waals interactions in chalcogenides. For incommensurate phases, we will employ the superspace approximation and the frozen-phonon method to model dynamic disorder. Defect calculations using the PyCDT code will model intrinsic off-centering and trap states. Band unfolding techniques will be applied to extract effective band structures from large supercells of disordered and modulated structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-principles electronic structure map for these complex material families, directly linking specific structural motifs (lone-pair coordination, modulation) to band engineering outcomes. It will provide a computational design rule set for discovering new high-performance thermoelectrics and functional semiconductors within chalcogenide chemistry. The results will be validated against existing single-crystal diffraction and transport data from the group.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is standard, its application to model lone-pair-driven band structure and disorder-induced trap states in *incommensurately modulated* and *homologous* chalcogenide series is novel. This moves beyond modeling idealized periodic structures to address the real, complex structural features that define these materials' properties.

proposals.title: Controlling Lone Pair Stereochemical Activity and Photoluminescence via Quantum Confinement in Diamondoid Chalcogenide Nanocrystals
proposals.motivation: In bulk diamondoid semiconductors like AgInSe2, stereochemically active lone pairs from cations like Ag+ and In3+ create intrinsic off-centering, which strongly influences electronic structure, thermal transport, and photoluminescence (PL) quenching. However, the precise control of these lone pair-driven distortions in bulk materials is limited. Nanocrystals offer a powerful platform to manipulate such structural distortions through surface effects and quantum confinement, a strategy largely unexplored for these complex chalcogenides.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing nanocrystals of diamondoid AgInSe2 and related compounds will enable confinement and tuning of lone pair-driven distortions, thereby altering the conduction band structure to decouple electronic and thermal transport for thermoelectrics and modulating PL quenching mechanisms for potential optoelectronic applications.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phase-pure nanocrystals of AgInSe2 and its analogs using hot-injection colloidal methods, controlling size and surface chemistry. The local structure and off-centering will be probed with pair distribution function (PDF) analysis from synchrotron X-ray total scattering and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS). Electronic structure changes will be calculated using density functional theory (DFT) on model nanocrystal clusters. Thermoelectric properties (Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity) will be measured on pressed nanocrystal pellets, while PL dynamics will be studied via time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials design principle: using nanoscale confinement to directly engineer stereochemically active lone pair distortions. It will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between local structure, band engineering, and transport properties, potentially leading to new high-performance n-type thermoelectric nanomaterials and tunable infrared emitters based on non-toxic elements.
proposals.novelty: While Kanatzidis's group has extensively studied lone pair effects in bulk diamondoid structures, applying quantum confinement in nanocrystals to actively control these distortions and their functional consequences is a novel and unexplored direction that merges complex bulk crystal chemistry with nanoscale synthetic control.

proposals.title: Decoupling Charge and Heat Transport in Deliberately Low-Symmetry and Amorphous Chalcogenides
proposals.motivation: The prevailing paradigm in thermoelectrics (pre-2026) seeks high-performance materials through high crystal symmetry and complex unit cells to scatter phonons while maintaining good electronic bands. However, this approach may have intrinsic limits in decoupling electron and phonon transport. Recent work on incommensurate and homologous series compounds suggests that structural complexity itself, not necessarily high symmetry, is key. This proposal challenges the necessity of high symmetry by exploring if deliberately disordered phases can provide a superior platform for independent optimization of electrical conductivity and thermal insulation.
proposals.hypothesis: Deliberately synthesized low-symmetry, incommensurate, or amorphous phases in chalcogenide systems (e.g., based on Ag-In-Se or A-Ta-Se) will exhibit stronger phonon scattering from inherent structural disorder while preserving percolation pathways for charge carriers through localized 'crystal-like' short-range order or resonant bonding, leading to an enhanced thermoelectric figure of merit ZT.
proposals.method: We will synthesize candidate materials using molten flux and vapor transport techniques, targeting amorphous thin films and bulk glasses in the Ag-In-Se system, and extending to incommensurate A-Ta-Se analogs. Structural characterization will combine high-energy X-ray total scattering with pair distribution function (PDF) analysis and advanced electron microscopy to quantify short- and medium-range order. Electronic structure will be probed via ultra-violet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), complemented by density functional theory (DFT) calculations on model clusters. Simultaneous measurement of electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and thermal conductivity (via laser flash analysis and 3ω method) will evaluate transport decoupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational understanding of the thermoelectric transport physics in disordered chalcogenides, directly testing the symmetry-decoupling hypothesis. It will provide new design principles that move beyond crystalline perfection, potentially identifying a new class of high-performance thermoelectric materials. The synthesis-structure-property relationships defined will guide future exploration of amorphous and composite thermoelectrics.
proposals.novelty: While disorder is typically viewed as detrimental, we propose its deliberate, controlled introduction as a primary design tool. This inverts the standard crystalline materials optimization strategy and leverages the group's expertise in complex chalcogenides to explore uncharted structural territory for thermoelectrics.

proposals.title: High ZT via Low Mobility: Exploring Complex Chalcogenides with Intrinsically Low Electron Mobility and Ultralow Lattice Thermal Conductivity
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in n-type thermoelectric materials research (pre-2026) prioritizes high electron mobility to achieve high power factor. This often leads to a focus on simple, high-symmetry structures. However, the thermoelectric figure of merit ZT = S²σT/κ depends on both electronic (σ) and thermal (κ) transport. An alternative path to high ZT, largely underexplored, is to intentionally design materials where modest electrical conductivity from low-mobility carriers is compensated by an exceptionally low lattice thermal conductivity (κ_L).
proposals.hypothesis: Complex, low-symmetry chalcogenide crystals with inherently low electron mobility, due to heavy band effective mass and strong carrier scattering, can achieve high ZT if their lattice thermal conductivity is driven to the amorphous limit through hierarchical phonon scattering from incommensurate modulations, intrinsic site disorder, and anharmonic soft modes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize candidate materials from families known for ultralow κ_L and structural complexity, such as incommensurately modulated phases (e.g., A-Ta-Se systems) and diamondoid derivatives (e.g., Ag-In-Se systems). Single-crystal and polycrystalline samples will be characterized for crystal structure (single-crystal X-ray diffraction), band structure (density functional theory calculations), and charge/thermal transport (simultaneous measurement of Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, and thermal diffusivity from 300-800 K). The analysis will decouple electronic (mobility, effective mass via Hall effect) and lattice thermal contributions to validate the compensation mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete materials design strategy that inverts the conventional high-mobility priority, expanding the search space for high-performance thermoelectrics. It will provide a validated set of structural and electronic descriptors for identifying 'low-mobility, low-κ' materials, contributing a new chapter to the understanding of structure-property relationships in complex crystals for energy conversion.
proposals.novelty: While low thermal conductivity is widely sought, systematically targeting materials with intrinsically poor electron mobility as a beneficial starting point is a counter-intuitive design principle. It directly leverages Prof. Kanatzidis's expertise in complex, structurally diverse chalcogenides, which are typically avoided in high-mobility searches, to create a new paradigm for n-type thermoelectric optimization.

proposals.title: Temperature-Adaptive Thermoelectric Performance via Incommensurate Structural Modulation in Chalcogenide Solid Solutions
proposals.motivation: Thermoelectric materials exhibit peak performance within narrow temperature ranges, as their electronic and thermal transport properties are typically static. This limits efficiency for applications like waste heat recovery, where the heat source temperature fluctuates. Pre-2026 work on incommensurately modulated structures and homologous series in chalcogenides suggests a pathway to intrinsically temperature-responsive materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solid solutions between compounds with different, temperature-sensitive incommensurate modulations will exhibit adaptive electronic band structures and phonon scattering mechanisms, leading to self-optimized thermoelectric performance across a broad temperature range.
proposals.method: We will synthesize solid solutions between known incommensurate phases, such as A14+2/3Ta8Se46+2/3 (A = Rb, Cs) and related diamondoid compounds like AgInSe2. High-temperature single-crystal X-ray diffraction will map the evolution of modulation vectors and atomic off-centering with temperature. Temperature-dependent band structure calculations (DFT) and thermal transport measurements (κ_lat, σ, S) will be correlated to identify how specific structural adaptations (e.g., changing periodicity of modulations) tune the charge carrier effective mass and phonon mean free path.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for adaptive thermoelectrics, moving beyond fixed-property optimization. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how incommensurate structural features can be dynamically tuned by temperature to beneficially decouple electronic and thermal transport. The resulting materials could significantly broaden the operational temperature window of thermoelectric generators.
proposals.novelty: While structural modulation is known to affect properties, deliberately engineering it to be temperature-responsive for adaptive performance is a novel concept. This approach leverages Kanatzidis's expertise in complex chalcogenides to create a material whose intrinsic structure, not just extrinsic composition, changes functionally with operating conditions.

proposals.title: Designing High-Performance Thermoelectrics through Intrinsically Low Group Velocity Phonons in Complex Chalcogenide Structures
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for reducing lattice thermal conductivity (κ_L) in thermoelectrics relies on scattering phonons via defects, nanostructuring, and alloying. While effective, this approach often degrades electronic transport. A more fundamental strategy, targeting the phonon group velocity (v_g) itself—a key component of κ_L—remains underexplored. Pre-2026 work on complex, low-symmetry structures like incommensurately modulated phases and diamondoids suggests a rich playground for engineering phonon dispersions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that complex chalcogenide crystal structures with heavy, weakly bonded atoms, inherent structural modulations, and low elastic stiffness will host phonon bands with intrinsically low group velocities across a wide frequency range, leading to ultralow κ_L without extrinsic scattering centers.
proposals.method: We will employ first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations to screen candidate systems from known structure types in the Kanatzidis group portfolio, such as the A14+2/3Ta8Se46+2/3 family and diamondoid derivatives. Phonon band structures and group velocities will be computed using density functional perturbation theory (DFPT). This computational screening will guide the synthesis of targeted n-type and p-type chalcogenides via solid-state and molten flux methods. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction will determine precise atomic positions and modulations, while thermal transport (κ, C_p) and elastic property measurements on oriented single crystals will validate the low v_g hypothesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials design principle for low κ_L by directly engineering the phonon dispersion, moving beyond scattering-centric approaches. It will provide a concrete link between specific structural motifs (e.g., modulations, soft bonding networks) and suppressed phonon group velocities. The discovery of new high-zT materials with intrinsically low thermal conductivity will significantly advance thermoelectrics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically prioritizing intrinsic phonon slowness over defect scattering as the primary design criterion for low κ_L. This inverts the conventional optimization sequence and leverages the group's expertise in synthesizing complex, 'phonon-glass' crystal structures that are predicted to exhibit this property inherently.

proposals.title: Dynamically Modulating Thermal Transport via Reversible Structural Phase Transitions in Chalcogenide Solid Solutions
proposals.motivation: A central challenge in thermoelectrics is the inherent interdependence of electronic and thermal transport properties. The lattice thermal conductivity (κ_L) is typically a static, detrimental parameter that limits the figure of merit zT. Phase-change materials (PCMs), which undergo reversible, temperature-driven structural transitions, offer a novel paradigm for dynamically controlling κ_L within the operational temperature range of a device, potentially decoupling it from electronic transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing solid solutions based on chalcogenides with incommensurate or homologous structures, we can engineer a reversible, diffusionless structural phase transition that significantly reduces κ_L at elevated temperatures while preserving or enhancing the electronic power factor, leading to a temperature-averaged enhancement in thermoelectric performance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize A-site solid solutions in systems like (Rb,Cs)1+xTa8Se46+δ or explore diamondoid derivatives (e.g., (Ag,Cu)InSe2) to induce a temperature-tunable phase boundary between two distinct structure types (e.g., a commensurate and an incommensurately modulated phase). Crystal structure evolution will be characterized by temperature-dependent single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Electronic band structure calculations (DFT) and Boltzmann transport theory will model the electronic transport, while lattice dynamics (phonon dispersion) and anharmonic lattice models will be used to interpret temperature-dependent thermal conductivity measurements (laser flash analysis, PPMS) across the transition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials design principle for active thermal management in thermoelectrics by leveraging intrinsic structural instability. It will provide a foundational understanding of how phonon scattering mechanisms evolve through a reversible phase transition and demonstrate a pathway to achieve a temperature-dependent 'phonon glass' state that is dynamically activated.
proposals.novelty: While PCMs are explored for data storage, their application for dynamic thermal conductivity tuning in thermoelectrics is unexplored. The novelty lies in targeting materials with pre-existing complex structural motifs (incommensurate, homologous) from the Kanatzidis group's portfolio, where subtle compositional changes can trigger the required reversible transition without compromising electronic integrity.

proposals.title: Targeted Passivation of Dual Vacancy Defects in Halide Perovskites via Ligand Screening for Enhanced Thermoelectric Stability
proposals.motivation: Halide perovskites, such as methylammonium lead iodide (MAPbI3), show promise for thermoelectrics but suffer from intrinsic ionic defects like lead (Pb) and iodide (I) vacancies, which facilitate ion migration and degrade stability. Pre-2026 work has shown that monodentate ligands can passivate single surface defects, but correlated vacancy pairs remain a critical, unaddressed source of electronic trap states and thermal instability in the bulk and at grain boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rationally designed bifunctional or multidentate organic ligands can selectively bind to and passivate correlated vacancy defect pairs (e.g., Pb-I, or organic cation-halide), simultaneously suppressing ion migration and improving electronic transport properties.
proposals.method: We will employ first-principles density functional theory (DFT) screening, using the VASP code, to calculate binding energies of a curated library of diamine, dicarboxylate, and dithiol ligands to modeled defect pairs in MAPbI3 and CsPbI3. High-throughput calculations will assess the impact on defect formation energies, electronic band structure (focusing on trap state elimination), and phonon spectra. Promising candidates will be synthesized, and single crystals or dense polycrystalline pellets will be grown for validation of thermal transport (via laser flash analysis) and electronic transport (via Hall effect and Seebeck coefficient measurements).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for defect engineering in halide perovskites, moving beyond single-site passivation. It will produce specific ligand recommendations and demonstrate their efficacy in enhancing the thermal stability and thermoelectric power factor of lead-based and lead-free perovskite materials.
proposals.novelty: While defect passivation is known, the targeted computational design and experimental validation of ligands for *correlated vacancy pairs* is a novel strategy, distinct from common single-ion passivation, directly addressing a root cause of ion migration in these soft lattices.

proposals.title: Dual-Site Ligand Engineering for Interface Optimization in Chalcogenide-Based Hole-Transport Layers
proposals.motivation: The performance of solution-processed optoelectronic devices, such as perovskite solar cells, is critically limited by interfacial energy level misalignment and defect states at the hole-transport layer (HTL) interface. Pre-2026 work on chalcogenide thermoelectrics and semiconductors by Kanatzidis's group has demonstrated precise control over electronic structure and thermal transport via complex crystal chemistry, but this expertise has not been systematically applied to the molecular design of surface ligands for HTLs. Addressing this interface with tailored ligands offers a direct path to improved device efficiency and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing chalcogenide-based, dual-site organic-inorganic ligands—where one site binds to the nanocrystal surface and a second, conjugated site interacts with the polymeric HTL—will simultaneously passivate surface traps and minimize the valence band energy offset, leading to superior hole extraction.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of dual-site ligands featuring a primary anchoring group (e.g., thiolate, selenolate) for binding to p-type chalcogenide (e.g., SnS, Cu2ZnSnS4) nanocrystals, and a secondary functional group (e.g., carbazole, triphenylamine) tuned for energy alignment with common HTMs like spiro-OMeTAD or P3HT. The electronic structure (band edges, density of states) of ligand-nanocrystal complexes will be calculated using density functional theory (DFT). Thin films will be fabricated, and their interfacial energetics probed via ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and time-resolved photoluminescence quenching. Charge transport will be measured using field-effect transistor and space-charge-limited current architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials design principle for interfacial ligands that goes beyond simple passivation to actively engineer energy level alignment. It will produce a new class of hybrid organic-inorganic interface materials specifically for hole-transport applications, directly contributing to higher-performance, stable perovskite and quantum dot solar cells. The findings will bridge the fields of solid-state chalcogenide chemistry and thin-film device engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the group's deep knowledge of chalcogenide crystal and band structure to the rational design of bifunctional molecular ligands, creating a direct, chemically tunable link between an inorganic semiconductor and an organic HTL—a concept distinct from conventional single-anchor ligands or bulk heterojunction engineering.

proposals.title: Grain Boundary Engineering in 2D Polycrystalline SnSe Films for High Thermoelectric Performance
proposals.motivation: The highest thermoelectric figures of merit (zT) are consistently achieved in high-quality single crystals, such as SnSe, due to their ultralow thermal conductivity and favorable electronic band structure. However, single crystals are not suitable for scalable device fabrication. Polycrystalline materials offer a practical path but suffer from performance degradation at grain boundaries, which typically scatter charge carriers more effectively than phonons. This work addresses the critical materials challenge of designing polycrystalline thermoelectrics that retain the beneficial electronic and thermal transport properties of their single-crystal counterparts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by synthesizing 2D polycrystalline films of layered chalcogenides (e.g., SnSe, SnS) with controlled, large-area grain orientation and chemically passivated grain boundaries, we can achieve charge carrier mobility and a phonon scattering hierarchy approaching single-crystalline quality, leading to high zT in a scalable material form.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step vapor transport and thin-film deposition method to synthesize large-area, textured polycrystalline SnSe films. Grain boundary structure and chemistry will be controlled via post-synthesis annealing in controlled Se/Sn vapor and intentional doping (e.g., Na, Ag). Transport properties (electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, Hall mobility) and thermal conductivity (via time-domain thermoreflectance) will be measured in-plane and cross-plane. The experimental data will be correlated with detailed microstructural analysis using TEM and STEM-EELS to directly link boundary atomic structure and composition with carrier and phonon scattering rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive set of microstructural design rules for grain boundaries in layered thermoelectrics, distinguishing between boundaries that are detrimental, benign, or beneficial for zT. It will demonstrate a materials processing pathway to high-performance polycrystalline films, bridging the gap between fundamental single-crystal studies and applied thermoelectric device engineering.
proposals.novelty: While grain boundary engineering is known, systematically decoupling its effects on electronic and thermal transport in anisotropic 2D systems, and specifically targeting a 'single-crystal-like' transport regime in a polycrystalline film, is a novel and non-obvious strategy given the historical focus on bulk polycrystals or nanostructured composites for phonon scattering.

proposals.title: Engineering a Direct Band Gap in SnP2Se6 via Cation Substitution and Dimensional Reduction for Enhanced Optoelectronic Performance
proposals.motivation: SnP2Se6 is a promising layered semiconductor for photovoltaics and light emission, but its indirect band gap fundamentally limits optical absorption efficiency and radiative recombination rates. Overcoming this limitation would unlock its potential for high-performance, earth-abundant optoelectronic devices. This challenge aligns with the Kanatzidis group's expertise in manipulating structure-property relationships in chalcogenide materials to control electronic and thermal transport.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted chemical substitution (e.g., with Ge, Pb) and controlled dimensional reduction from 3D to 2D-like structures can modify the orbital character at the valence and conduction band edges, shifting the band extrema to the same k-point and creating a direct or quasi-direct band gap in SnP2Se6 derivatives.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and synthetic approach. First, density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) will screen candidate substitutions (A = Ge, Pb; B = As, Sb) in A(P2Se6) and assess dimensional confinement effects on band structure. Guided by computations, single crystals of promising compositions (e.g., Sn1-xGexP2Se6, SnP2Se6-ySey) will be synthesized via high-temperature solid-state and chemical vapor transport methods. Their crystal structures will be determined via single-crystal X-ray diffraction, and electronic structure will be probed via temperature-dependent resistivity, Seebeck coefficient, and optical absorption spectroscopy to validate the direct gap transition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials design principle for converting indirect to direct gap semiconductors through synergistic chemical and structural tuning. It will produce a new family of tunable direct-gap chalcogenides with potential for thin-film photovoltaics and LEDs. The study will also provide fundamental insights into the relationship between local coordination geometry, dimensionality, and band convergence.
proposals.novelty: While band engineering is common, a focused strategy to specifically invert the indirect gap of the SnP2Se6 structure type by leveraging the group's mastery of modulated and homologous chalcogenide series is unexplored. The approach integrates dimensional control—a known factor for direct gaps in 2D materials—with the chemical complexity of 3D network structures.

proposals.title: Quantum Confinement and Bandgap Engineering in Monolayer SnP2Se6: A Pathway to Enhanced Photovoltaic Response
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on bulk and few-layer SnP2Se6 identifies it as a promising van der Waals semiconductor with a suitable bandgap for visible-light absorption and intrinsic ferroelectricity, relevant for photovoltaic and optoelectronic applications. However, the precise evolution of its electronic structure upon reduction to a true monolayer, where quantum confinement effects are maximized, remains unexplored. Understanding this is critical for designing ultra-thin, high-efficiency solar cells and light-emitting devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that isolating a single monolayer of SnP2Se6 will induce significant quantum confinement, leading to a direct and widened bandgap, which will dramatically enhance its photovoltaic properties, including the absorption coefficient and the theoretical Shockley-Queisser efficiency limit.
proposals.method: High-quality single crystals of SnP2Se6 will be synthesized via chemical vapor transport. Monolayers will be isolated using mechanical exfoliation and identified via optical contrast and atomic force microscopy. The crystal structure will be verified with selected-area electron diffraction. Electronic band structure calculations will be performed using density functional theory (DFT) with the HSE06 hybrid functional to predict the confinement-induced bandgap shift. Experimental validation will come from temperature-dependent in-plane thermal and electrical transport measurements on the monolayer, and crucially, from direct bandgap determination via low-temperature photoluminescence and UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy on supported and suspended flakes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property relationship for monolayer SnP2Se6, quantifying the quantum confinement effect on its bandgap and providing essential transport parameters. It will deliver a foundational assessment of its potential as a photovoltaic absorber in an atomically thin form, guiding the design of novel 2D heterostructure solar cells.
proposals.novelty: While Kanatzidis's group has extensively studied bulk and complex 3D thermoelectric and photovoltaic materials, applying this materials discovery and deep characterization expertise to probe the quantum-confined limit of a specific, promising 2D photovoltaic semiconductor (SnP2Se6) is a novel direction. The combination of single-crystal synthesis, monolayer isolation, and correlated theoretical/experimental electronic structure analysis is a unique approach for this material system.

proposals.title: Probing Local Symmetry Breaking and Defects in Complex Chalcogenides via the Bulk Photovoltaic Effect
proposals.motivation: The bulk photovoltaic effect (BPVE) generates a steady-state photocurrent in non-centrosymmetric crystals under uniform illumination, with its magnitude and direction dictated by crystal symmetry and electronic structure. For complex, structurally diverse materials like the chalcogenides studied by the Kanatzidis group, subtle local symmetry deviations from the average structure—due to incommensurate modulations, off-centering, or defects—profoundly influence thermal and electronic transport but are challenging to characterize. A method to directly probe these local asymmetries via a functional electronic response is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The magnitude and polarization-dependent direction of the BPVE-generated photocurrent will serve as a highly sensitive, in-situ probe of local crystal symmetry and defect configurations in complex thermoelectric chalcogenides, correlating directly with deviations from ideal structure models that impact electronic band structure and thermal transport.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single crystals of target materials (e.g., diamondoid AgInSe2 and incommensurate A-Ta-Se phases) using established vapor transport techniques. High-sensitivity, vector-resolved photocurrent measurements will be performed on oriented single crystals using a focused, tunable-wavelength laser and a calibrated probe station. The experimental photocurrent tensor will be compared to first-principles calculations (DFT) of the shift current—the dominant BPVE mechanism—for both the ideal average crystal structure and models incorporating known modulations or intrinsic off-centering. Transport property measurements (Seebeck, conductivity) on the same crystals will establish thermoelectric performance correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish BPVE as a novel characterization tool for complex materials, providing a direct functional link between local symmetry breaking and electronic response. It will yield new insights into the structure-property relationships governing high-performance thermoelectrics, particularly the role of subtle structural distortions. The methodology could become a standard for assessing non-centrosymmetry and defect states in emerging energy materials.
proposals.novelty: While BPVE is studied in classic ferroelectrics, its application as a sensitive symmetry probe for complex, structurally modulated chalcogenides is unexplored. The approach inverts the typical problem—instead of designing materials for BPVE, we use BPVE to decode material structure—creating a novel diagnostic tool grounded in the group's expertise in crystal chemistry and transport.

proposals.title: Decoupling Lattice and Electronic Pressure in Diamondoid Thermoelectrics via Competing Epitaxial Strain and Isovalent Doping
proposals.motivation: In complex chalcogenides like AgInSe2, the intertwined effects of lattice strain and electronic pressure from doping obscure the fundamental drivers of thermoelectric performance. Pre-2026 work shows these materials are sensitive to both structural distortions and band structure engineering, but their individual contributions are conflated in bulk studies, hindering rational design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying independent, competing epitaxial strain and isovalent doping to thin-film diamondoid chalcogenides will decouple their effects, revealing that electronic pressure from orbital hybridization, not just lattice constant changes, is the primary lever for optimizing the thermoelectric power factor.
proposals.method: We will fabricate combinatorial thin-film libraries of isovalently doped (e.g., Ga/In, S/Se) AgInSe2 and related diamondoid compounds using pulsed laser deposition. These will be grown on a single, compositionally graded substrate (e.g., (Ba,Sr)TiO3) to impose a continuous in-plane epitaxial strain gradient. High-throughput characterization will map structural (XRD), electronic (temperature-dependent Hall effect), and thermal transport (time-domain thermoreflectance) properties across the dual-parameter space. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations will model the strained, doped structures to interpret the experimental band structure and transport trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first experimental phase diagram separating lattice and electronic pressure effects in complex thermoelectrics. It will establish design rules for selectively tuning carrier mobility and effective mass, directly informing the synthesis of next-generation high-zT materials. The combinatorial thin-film platform will serve as a model for decoupling correlated variables in other functional materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the adversarial experimental design—actively competing two independent tuning knobs (external strain vs. internal chemical pressure) that are typically varied in concert, enabling their true individual roles to be isolated and quantified for the first time.

proposals.title: Decoupling Electronic and Structural Pressure in Complex Chalcogenides via Topological Dopant Design
proposals.motivation: In solid-state chemistry, chemical doping typically couples electronic effects (e.g., carrier concentration, band filling) with structural effects (lattice strain from ionic size mismatch), confounding the study of pure electronic correlations. This entanglement limits the ability to independently tune electronic structure for optimizing properties like thermoelectric performance or inducing quantum phase transitions. A method to exert 'pure' electronic pressure would provide a powerful new knob for materials design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dopants can be strategically placed in electronically coupled but structurally buffered sites within complex, low-dimensional chalcogenide frameworks to exert dominant electronic pressure via orbital interactions, with minimal direct lattice strain.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput DFT calculations (VASP) to screen candidate dopants (e.g., transition metals, post-transition metals) in structurally diverse host systems, such as the A14+2/3Ta8Se46+2/3 family and diamondoid AgInSe2, focusing on sites within modular or chain-like substructures. Screening criteria will prioritize electronic structure metrics (band filling, orbital symmetry breaking, Fermi surface distortion) over ionic radius. Promising candidates will be synthesized via high-temperature solid-state and molten salt techniques, with structural decoupling validated by single-crystal X-ray diffraction and electronic effects probed by transport and spectroscopic measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for doping that separates electronic and structural perturbations. It will provide a testbed for studying electronic correlations and band engineering in thermoelectrics without the confounding variable of lattice contraction. The resulting materials could exhibit novel transport regimes or enhanced thermoelectric figures of merit.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting the biological concept of allosteric regulation to solid-state doping, moving beyond the conventional paradigm of ionic size substitution. It leverages complex, modulated crystal structures as platforms where electronic and structural effects can be spatially separated.

proposals.title: Decoupling Electronic and Lattice Effects in Thermoelectric Materials via Isovalent Chemical Pressure
proposals.motivation: In thermoelectric materials, chemical doping simultaneously alters electronic structure and lattice parameters, making it difficult to isolate the factors governing carrier mobility and phonon scattering. Pre-2026 work, including Kanatzidis's studies on diamondoid and incommensurate structures, highlights the critical role of lattice dynamics and band convergence, but a systematic decoupling of 'chemical pressure' from physical lattice shrinkage is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that isovalent doping with ions of similar size but higher electronegativity can exert 'electronic pressure'—modifying band structure and carrier effective mass—without significantly reducing the lattice constant, thereby enabling the independent optimization of electronic and thermal transport properties.
proposals.method: We will target n-type AgInSe2 and related diamondoid chalcogenides. Using single-crystal growth and precise chemical substitution (e.g., S for Se, or controlled Ga/In mixing), we will synthesize a series where the dopant ionic radius is conserved. Crystal structure will be determined via single-crystal X-ray diffraction to monitor lattice parameters. Electronic band structure will be calculated using density functional theory (DFT) to track changes in conduction band curvature and density of states. Thermal and electronic transport properties (Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity) will be measured on oriented single crystals to correlate performance with structural and electronic changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new doping strategy to selectively tune electronic bands without detrimental lattice compression, providing a clearer design rule for high-efficiency thermoelectrics. It will yield fundamental insights into the separate roles of chemical pressure and strain in complex chalcogenides, directly informing the search for materials with high power factor and low lattice thermal conductivity.
proposals.novelty: While chemical pressure is a known concept, its application specifically to decouple electronic and lattice effects in thermoelectrics via isovalent, size-conserved doping is unexplored. This approach moves beyond conventional aliovalent doping which inherently couples charge carrier introduction with lattice strain.

proposals.title: Engineering Topological Band Distortions via Orbital and Lattice Symmetry Manipulation for High-Performance Thermoelectrics
proposals.motivation: The field of thermoelectrics has heavily focused on band convergence through incremental chemical tuning, often yielding diminishing returns. A paradigm shift is needed to achieve step-change improvements in the power factor.
