This proposal posits that radical, topology-driven band engineering, inspired by the concept of genetic network rewiring in evolution, offers a more powerful route to superior electronic transport than simple energetic proximity.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted manipulation of specific orbital hybridization patterns and symmetry-breaking lattice distortions, rather than just chemical composition, can induce extreme band distortions or emergent band features that create a qualitatively superior electronic structure for thermoelectric performance, surpassing the limits of conventional band convergence.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput density functional theory (DFT) calculations to screen chalcogenide and diamondoid structure families (e.g., AgInSe2 derivatives, A-Ta-Se systems) for candidates with strong intrinsic spin-orbit coupling or soft phonon modes. Using the VASP and BoltzTraP2 codes, we will compute band structures and transport properties under controlled symmetry reduction (via strain and hypothetical cation ordering) to map the 'toolkit' of interactions. Promising theoretical targets will be synthesized as single crystals, with their modulated structures characterized via single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Electronic structure validation will be performed via angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) and correlated with thermal and electrical transport measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for thermoelectric materials based on topological band engineering, moving beyond the convergence paradigm. It will produce a map of specific structural and orbital interactions that lead to beneficial band distortions. The project aims to deliver new synthetic compounds with validated, non-trivial band structures that exhibit enhanced power factors.
proposals.novelty: The approach prioritizes the fundamental mechanisms that *generate* band topology over the incremental tuning of existing bands, a conceptual shift analogous to developmental biology's focus on gene regulatory networks over individual traits. It explicitly connects symmetry-breaking structural motifs (like incommensurate modulations) to the engineering of radical electronic structure features.

proposals.title: Phononic Interference Coatings: Constructive Phonon Transport in Engineered Chalcogenide Superlattices
proposals.motivation: In thermoelectrics, the dominant paradigm is to minimize lattice thermal conductivity (κ_L) via point defects, rattlers, and nanostructuring to scatter a broad spectrum of phonons. This approach inherently limits the electronic power factor due to increased carrier scattering. A new strategy to selectively enhance phonon transport in a targeted frequency window could decouple phonon and electron transport paths, enabling independent optimization of electronic and thermal properties for higher zT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that atomically precise, discordant heterointerfaces in chalcogenide superlattices can be designed to act as phononic interference coatings, creating constructive interference for specific mid-to-long-wavelength phonon modes, thereby increasing their group velocity and creating a thermal conductivity passband.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of epitaxial [(AgInSe2)_m/(SnSe)_n] superlattices, leveraging Kanatzidis's expertise in diamondoid and layered chalcogenides. Layer thickness (m, n) will be systematically varied to match predicted phonon wavelengths from lattice dynamics calculations. In-plane and cross-plane thermal transport will be measured using time-domain thermoreflectance. The experimental data will be compared to non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) simulations of phonon transport across the interfaces, explicitly modeling the scattering phase shifts to identify constructive interference conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials design principle of 'phonon band engineering' beyond scattering. It will provide the first experimental evidence for interface-enhanced phonon transport, challenging the universal 'interface-as-barrier' model. If successful, it will introduce a novel lever for selectively tuning κ_L to benefit thermoelectric and thermal management applications.
proposals.novelty: The concept directly inverts the standard goal of phonon scattering by seeking constructive interference, a radical shift inspired by photonics but unexplored in phonon engineering. It moves beyond random alloy scattering to use precisely ordered, discordant atomic layers as coherent phononic elements.

proposals.title: Chemical Pressure-Induced Electronic Phase Transitions in Te-Site Doped SnTe: A Search for Non-Monotonic Transport and Topological Evolution
proposals.motivation: SnTe is a well-studied thermoelectric and topological crystalline insulator where cation (Sn-site) doping is common, but anion (Te-site) doping remains underexplored as a tool for applying chemical pressure. Pre-2026 work on homologous series and incommensurate structures by Kanatzidis's group demonstrates that subtle chemical substitutions can induce complex structural modulations and dramatic property changes. Mapping the electronic response of SnTe to systematic Te-site alloying could reveal hidden phase boundaries critical for optimizing thermoelectric performance and controlling topological states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemical pressure from heavy Te-site substitution (e.g., with Se, S) in SnTe drives the system through subtle structural or electronic phase transitions, leading to non-monotonic changes in band structure, carrier effective mass, and potentially topological character at critical doping thresholds.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a dense series of SnTe1-xXx (X = Se, S) single crystals via modified Bridgman or vapor transport methods. High-resolution structural analysis (single-crystal X-ray diffraction, pair distribution function analysis) will probe for local distortions and incommensurate modulations. Electronic structure will be calculated using density functional theory (DFT) with the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP), focusing on band convergence and topological invariants. These will be correlated with detailed temperature-dependent measurements of electrical resistivity, Hall effect, Seebeck coefficient, and spectroscopic probes (ARPES, optical conductivity).
proposals.contribution: This work will construct the first detailed electronic phase diagram of SnTe under anion-site chemical pressure, identifying specific doping levels where property optima occur. It will establish Te-site doping as a viable strategy for inducing electronic phase transitions in IV-VI semiconductors, potentially uncovering new correlated or topological phases relevant for next-generation thermoelectrics and quantum materials.
proposals.novelty: While cation doping in SnTe is well-known, the deliberate search for non-monotonic electronic structure evolution driven by anion-site chemical pressure is novel. The approach integrates Kanatzidis's expertise in complex chalcogenide structures and thermoelectric transport to probe for hidden phase transitions in a canonical system.

proposals.title: Chemical Pressure via Te-Site Alloying in SnSe: A Novel Route to Enhance Thermoelectric Performance
proposals.motivation: The high thermoelectric performance of SnSe arises from its ultralow thermal conductivity and complex electronic structure. Prior strategies have focused on doping the Sn site to optimize carrier concentration. However, this approach can perturb the unique anharmonic bonding and band structure responsible for its exceptional properties. Exploring chemical pressure via the anion site offers an alternative, potentially less disruptive path to tune the lattice and electronic transport simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: Heavy alloying or doping on the Te site in SnSe (e.g., forming SnSe1-xTex or SnSe1-xSx) will apply chemical pressure, systematically modifying the crystal structure, increasing band degeneracy, and further reducing lattice thermal conductivity without degrading the favorable electronic band features inherited from the SnSe matrix.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single crystals of SnSe1-xXx (X = Te, S) using the Bridgman method to ensure high-quality samples for definitive transport measurements. Crystal structure and incommensurate modulations will be characterized via single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Electronic band structure calculations (DFT) will map the evolution of band convergence with alloying. Temperature-dependent measurements of electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and thermal conductivity (using laser flash analysis and steady-state methods) will provide the full thermoelectric transport matrix.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish anion-site alloying as a viable and powerful strategy for performance enhancement in SnSe and related materials, moving beyond the conventional cation-doping paradigm. It will provide a detailed understanding of how chemical pressure affects the interplay between anharmonicity, band structure, and thermal transport in complex chalcogenides.
proposals.novelty: While cation doping in SnSe is well-studied, targeted heavy alloying on the chalcogen site to induce chemical pressure is largely unexplored. This approach leverages the group's expertise in complex, modulated structures to intentionally engineer disorder and strain on the anion sublattice, a non-obvious tactic given the historical focus on the Sn site.

proposals.title: Mapping the Synthesis-Structure-Property Landscape in CdS-Alloyed Thermoelectrics via a Multi-Parameter Design of Experiments
proposals.motivation: Thermoelectric performance optimization in materials like AgInSe2 and complex chalcogenides often focuses on composition alone, while synthesis and processing are treated as fixed variables. This leads to non-reproducible results and obscures the true potential of alloying strategies. A systematic study that co-optimizes composition with fabrication pathways is essential to develop robust, high-performance materials suitable for scalable production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optimal thermoelectric figure of merit (zT) for a CdS-alloyed system is not defined by a single alloying fraction, but by a specific, synergistic combination of composition, synthesis method, and post-processing that simultaneously optimizes electronic band structure and phonon scattering microstructure.
proposals.method: We will employ a 3-factor, 2-level full factorial design of experiments, varying CdS alloying fraction (x), synthesis route (solid-state reaction vs. melt growth), and annealing protocol (temperature/time). Samples will be characterized by powder XRD and SEM for phase purity and microstructure. Electronic structure will be probed via UV-Vis spectroscopy and temperature-dependent Hall/Seebeck measurements, while phonon scattering will be assessed via temperature-dependent thermal conductivity (laser flash) and Raman spectroscopy. Multi-variable regression will correlate inputs to outputs (zT, carrier mobility, lattice thermal conductivity).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a quantitative, transferable map linking processing variables to functional properties in a model thermoelectric system. It will establish clear, reproducible protocols for achieving peak performance and elucidate the microstructural origins of enhanced phonon scattering beyond simple alloying. The methodology will serve as a blueprint for robust optimization in other complex chalcogenides.
proposals.novelty: Unlike typical composition-only studies, this approach treats synthesis and processing as active, co-equal design variables, explicitly decoupling their effects on electronic and thermal transport to identify truly robust performance maxima.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Computational Screening for Chemical Pressure Dopants in SnTe: Accelerating the Discovery of High-Efficiency Thermoelectrics
proposals.motivation: SnTe is a promising mid-temperature thermoelectric material, but its performance is limited by a large energy offset (ΔE) between the L and Σ valence bands, which hinders the beneficial convergence needed for a high Seebeck coefficient. Pre-2026 work has shown that small-radius dopants like Cd and Mg can apply 'chemical pressure' to reduce ΔE, but the exploration of the vast chemical space for other, potentially superior dopants remains slow and empirical. A systematic, theory-guided approach is urgently needed to efficiently identify optimal dopants that maximize band convergence and power factor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput computational screening of isoelectronic and aliovalent dopants, based on strict ionic radius and electronic structure criteria, will reveal novel, high-performance dopants for SnTe by accurately predicting their incorporation stability and their efficacy in reducing the L-Σ band offset.
proposals.method: We will define a search space of potential dopants from Groups 2, 12, and select transition metals, filtering by ionic radius (< ~1 Å) and valence state. Using the high-throughput framework of the Materials Project and pymatgen, we will perform automated density functional theory (DFT) calculations with the VASP code. Key computed properties for each dopant in a SnTe supercell will include: formation energy, induced lattice strain, electronic band structure (specifically ΔE), and the thermoelectric power factor estimated via the constant relaxation-time approximation within BoltzTraP.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a ranked shortlist of theoretically optimal dopants for SnTe, directly guiding targeted experimental synthesis. It will establish a quantitative structure-property relationship for chemical pressure effects in rock-salt chalcogenides and deliver new, high-performance SnTe-based compositions with enhanced thermoelectric figures of merit (zT).
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput screening is established in catalysis, its targeted application to the specific problem of chemical pressure and band convergence in thermoelectrics is novel. This approach moves beyond trial-and-error doping by systematically evaluating both isoelectronic and aliovalent substitutions based on first-principles transport properties, a strategy not comprehensively applied to SnTe before 2026.

proposals.title: Mapping Local Structural Distortions to Multifunctional Properties in Complex Chalcogenides: Chemical Pressure as a Design Tool
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the Kanatzidis group has shown that complex chalcogenides (e.g., AgInSe2, A-Ta-Se phases) exhibit rich structural chemistry, including incommensurate modulations and off-centering, which critically influence thermal and electronic transport. However, the direct causal link between chemically induced local lattice distortions (strain, off-centering) and the simultaneous evolution of mechanical robustness and optoelectronic response remains unexplored, limiting the rational design of multifunctional materials for thermoelectrics and optoelectronics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemical pressure, via controlled doping or stoichiometric variation in selected chalcogenide systems, creates specific, predictable local structural motifs (e.g., distorted polyhedra, strain gradients) that directly and concurrently govern macroscopic fracture toughness and localized optoelectronic properties like photoluminescence efficiency and spectral position.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single crystals of tunable chalcogenide systems (e.g., (Ag,Cu)InSe2, Rb/Cs-Ta-Se homologues) to apply chemical pressure. Local structure will be probed via pair distribution function (PDF) analysis of synchrotron X-ray total scattering and high-resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM). These will be spatially correlated with property maps: nanoindentation for local modulus/hardness, conductive-atomic force microscopy (c-AFM) for electronic heterogeneity, and confocal photoluminescence (PL) microscopy for spectral shifts and quantum yield. Multivariate analysis will correlate specific distortion motifs with property clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new structure-property paradigm that moves beyond average crystal structure to define design rules based on local chemical environments. It will deliver a materials database linking specific distortion types to multifunctional performance, guiding the synthesis of next-generation materials where mechanical durability and electronic efficiency are co-optimized, such as in robust thermoelectric modules or fracture-resistant solar absorbers.
proposals.novelty: While local structure's role in electronic transport is recognized, its simultaneous and deterministic control over fracture mechanics is a novel concept. The integrated, spatially resolved experimental approach to directly correlate angstrom-scale distortions with micron-scale mechanical and optoelectronic properties represents a significant methodological advance beyond conventional bulk measurements.

proposals.title: Element-Resolved Lattice Dynamics in Complex Chalcogenides: Disentangling Local Mechanisms of Phonon Softening for Enhanced Thermoelectric Performance
proposals.motivation: In complex thermoelectric materials like diamondoid compounds and incommensurate structures studied by the Kanatzidis group, 'phonon-glass electron-crystal' behavior is crucial for low thermal conductivity. However, the precise local mechanisms by which specific 'discordant' atoms induce phonon softening—be it through local strain, charge transfer, or dynamic disorder—remain obscured in conventional, volume-averaged measurements. Deconvolving these mechanisms is essential for the rational design of next-generation thermoelectrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the phonon softening observed in complex chalcogenides with mixed or modulated structures can be quantitatively decomposed into distinct, element-specific contributions arising from local lattice strain, electronic band structure changes near the Fermi level, and the formation of dynamic short-range order.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-modal scattering suite on model systems (e.g., AgInSe2 derivatives and A-Ta-Se phases). Inelastic neutron scattering (SPINS, SEQUOIA) will map full phonon dispersions. Element-specific phonon densities of states will be obtained via resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) at synchrotron beamlines. Local structure and dynamics will be probed by temperature-dependent XAFS. A machine-learning framework, using a graph neural network (GNN) architecture trained on DFT-calculated force constants, will correlate the multi-modal experimental inputs to deconvolve the softening mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between specific atomic-scale perturbations and macroscopic lattice dynamics, moving beyond phenomenological models. It will provide a validated, element-specific design rule set for phonon engineering in complex semiconductors, directly informing the synthesis of new high-performance thermoelectric materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tightly integrated, simultaneous application of INS, RIXS, and XAFS to the same samples, coupled with a GNN-based analysis framework designed to handle the multi-scale, multi-modal data. This approach moves beyond identifying softening to assigning its precise atomic origin, a capability not demonstrated pre-2026.

proposals.title: Strain-Engineered Doping in Diamondoid Chalcogenides: A High-Throughput Computational and Epitaxial Synthesis Strategy for Enhanced Thermoelectric Performance
proposals.motivation: The performance of thermoelectric materials like AgInSe2 is critically governed by the interplay between doping and lattice strain, which independently tune electronic bands and phonon transport. Pre-2026, high-throughput computational screening has largely treated these variables separately, while experimental strain application via epitaxy has been exploratory rather than guided by predictive models. A synergistic, closed-loop approach is needed to systematically discover optimal dopant-strain pairs that are otherwise inaccessible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that programmable epitaxial strain, when guided by high-throughput density functional theory (DFT) screening across a continuous strain space, will reveal and enable the realization of novel dopant-strain configurations that synergistically optimize the thermoelectric power factor and reduce lattice thermal conductivity in diamondoid and related chalcogenides.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput DFT calculations using the VASP software with the SCAN meta-GGA functional to screen n-type (e.g., Cl, Br) and p-type (e.g., Na, K) dopants in AgInSe2 and related compounds across a range of biaxial strains (-4% to +4%). The BoltzTraP code will be used to compute thermoelectric transport coefficients. Predicted optimal dopant-strain pairs will be experimentally realized via molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) on mismatched substrates (e.g., MgO, SrTiO3) to impose the target strain, followed by structural (single-crystal X-ray diffraction) and property (thermal and electrical transport) characterization to validate predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational-experimental feedback loop for strain-engineered doping, directly leading to new, high-performance thermoelectric thin-film materials. It will produce a validated database of dopant effectiveness under strain, providing general design principles for manipulating charge and heat transport in complex chalcogenides.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, high-throughput screening of dopants *as a continuous function of programmable strain*, moving beyond static lattice calculations to directly guide epitaxial synthesis towards predicted optimal states, a strategy not yet systematically applied to complex thermoelectric chalcogenides.

proposals.title: Coherent Strain and Quantum Confinement in Epitaxial SnTe Quantum Dot Superlattices: A Dual-Parameter Pathway to Engineered Topological Surface States
proposals.motivation: Topological crystalline insulators (TCIs) like SnTe offer protected surface states for low-dissipation electronics, but their Dirac cone properties and phase stability are sensitive to strain and dimensionality. Pre-2026, tuning these properties in scalable thin-film architectures remains a challenge, as conventional methods like chemical doping or substrate clamping offer limited, non-uniform control. A platform that independently and uniformly controls strain and quantum confinement is needed to unlock tailored topological interfaces for devices.
proposals.hypothesis: Epitaxial growth of monodisperse SnTe quantum dots on lattice-mismatched substrates will impose a coherent, biaxial strain field across a superlattice, providing uniform chemical pressure to tune the TCI phase, while quantum confinement from the dot size independently modulates the band structure, enabling precise control over Dirac cone velocity and surface state spatial overlap.
proposals.method: We will synthesize colloidal SnTe quantum dots with precise size control. Using ligand-exchange and solution-processing techniques, we will assemble these dots into highly ordered superlattices epitaxially on single-crystal CdTe and PbSe substrates, leveraging pre-2026 expertise in nanocrystal assembly and the group's mastery of chalcogenide crystal growth. The strain state and structural coherence will be characterized by high-resolution XRD and TEM. Electronic structure and topological surface states will be probed via temperature-dependent electronic transport measurements (focusing on magnetoresistance and Hall effect) and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) on transferred films.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials platform for topological electronics by demonstrating dual-parameter tuning of TCI properties in a scalable thin-film format. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay of epitaxial strain and quantum confinement in topological materials. The resulting epitaxial quantum dot superlattices could serve as a testbed for novel device concepts in low-power electronics and quantum information based on engineered topological interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While strain and confinement have been studied separately in topological materials, their combined, independent exploitation within a single, coherent epitaxial architecture—achieved through quantum dot superlattice assembly—is a novel and non-obvious synthesis strategy. This approach moves beyond bulk crystals or continuous films to create a new class of 'topological metamaterials' with designer properties.

proposals.title: Quantifying Chemical Pressure-Driven Phonon Softening in Complex SnTe Alloys via Resonant Ultrasound Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: The thermoelectric figure of merit, zT, is limited by the lattice thermal conductivity (κ_l). In SnTe-based alloys, alloying with elements like Mn, Pb, or Sr introduces chemical pressure and complex defect structures that scatter phonons, but the specific phonon modes affected and the quantitative link between local strain and elastic softening remain poorly understood. A direct, compositionally resolved experimental probe of these effects is needed to move beyond empirical optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic alloying in ternary/quaternary SnTe systems (e.g., SnTe-MnTe-PbTe, SnTe-SrTe-NaSbTe2) induces specific, measurable softening of low-energy acoustic phonon modes via resonant ultrasound spectroscopy (RUS), and this softening directly correlates with the reduction in κ_l by enhancing phonon scattering.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single crystals of compositionally graded SnTe-based alloys using established vapor transport and Bridgman techniques. For each composition, we will measure the full elastic tensor (C_ij) non-destructively using resonant ultrasound spectroscopy (RUS), fitting the resonant frequencies of parallelepiped samples to determine stiffness coefficients. This data will be correlated with first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations of phonon dispersion and with direct measurements of thermal transport (κ_l) and electronic structure (Hall effect, Seebeck coefficient) on the same crystals.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative 'map' linking specific alloy-induced chemical pressure to measurable elastic softening and reduced κ_l in complex chalcogenides. It will provide a direct experimental metric for phonon engineering, enabling the rational design of high-zT materials by identifying alloy combinations that maximally soften targeted phonon modes without degrading electronic mobility.
proposals.novelty: While RUS is established for simple systems, its application to map phonon softening in complex, chemically pressurized ternary/quaternary thermoelectric alloys is novel. This approach moves beyond correlating bulk properties to directly probe the fundamental lattice dynamical changes induced by specific defect configurations.

proposals.title: Direct Atomic-Scale Correlation of Chemical Heterogeneity and Phonon Softening in Nanostructured GeTe-PbTe Thermoelectrics
proposals.motivation: Nanostructuring is a proven strategy to reduce lattice thermal conductivity (κ_L) in thermoelectrics by scattering phonons, but the precise atomic-scale chemical mechanisms behind specific phonon mode softening remain obscured by ensemble-averaging techniques. Establishing a direct, spatially resolved link between 3D chemical identity and local vibrational dynamics is critical for the rational design of next-generation high-efficiency materials. This problem sits at the heart of Kanatzidis's research on complex chalcogenides, where subtle structural modulations and off-centering dictate thermal transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local chemical environment of Ge-rich nanoprecipitates and their interfaces in a (GeTe)_(1-x)(PbTe)_x matrix directly governs the softening of specific low-energy acoustic and optical phonon modes, which is the primary driver of the suppressed κ_L in these high-performance thermoelectrics.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single-phase and nanostructured (GeTe)_(0.8)(PbTe)_0.2 alloys. Atom Probe Tomography (APT) will provide 3D nanoscale compositional maps, quantifying solute segregation and interfacial width at the atomic scale. Inelastic Neutron Scattering (INS) at the Spallation Neutron Source will measure the full phonon dispersion on specimens from the same batch. The APT chemical data will inform supercell construction for density functional theory (DFT) lattice dynamics calculations, enabling the deconvolution of the INS signal into contributions from the matrix, precipitate core, and strained interface regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind, direct experimental correlation between 3D atomic-scale chemistry and specific softened phonon modes in a functional material. It will move beyond phenomenological 'phonon scattering' models to a mechanistic, chemical-bond-level understanding of thermal transport suppression. The methodology will provide a generalizable blueprint for optimizing nanostructured thermoelectrics and other phonon-engineered materials.
proposals.novelty: While APT and INS are mature techniques, their synergistic combination to directly link a 3D atomic chemical map to a site-specific interpretation of phonon dynamics in a single material system is novel. This closes the loop between nanoscale heterogeneity and macroscopic property measurement, transforming chemical complexity from noise into a design parameter.

proposals.title: Quantifying Confinement-Induced Phonon Softening in SnTe Nanocrystals: An Inelastic Neutron Scattering Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that SnTe is a promising thermoelectric and topological material, where enhanced performance is linked to phonon softening and anharmonicity. However, the fundamental mechanisms of how quantum confinement and internal pressure modify specific phonon modes remain poorly understood, as bulk and thin-film studies cannot isolate the effects of discrete size reduction and surface disorder. Direct measurement of phonon dispersion in size-controlled nanocrystals is critical to validate theoretical models and guide the design of nanostructured materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that quantum confinement in SnTe nanocrystals induces a size-dependent internal chemical pressure, leading to a direct, measurable softening of transverse optical (TO) phonon modes, and that this softening can be systematically tuned and quantified by varying nanocrystal size and surface ligand chemistry.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of monodisperse, colloidal SnTe nanocrystals with diameters from 5 to 20 nm using hot-injection methods, with surface chemistry controlled by organic ligands (e.g., oleic acid) and inorganic chalcogenidometallate clusters. High-resolution inelastic neutron scattering (INS) measurements will be performed on gram-scale, solvent-free powders of these nanocrystals using spectrometers like ARCS at ORNL or IN5 at ILL to obtain the phonon density of states and dispersion relations. Data analysis will employ density functional perturbation theory (DFPT) calculations on model nanocrystal structures to assign modes and extract mode-specific Grüneisen parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental quantification of phonon softening and anharmonicity in quantum-confined SnTe, linking specific vibrational modes to confinement scale. It will establish design principles for manipulating phonon spectra via nanocrystal size and surface engineering, offering a new pathway to enhance thermoelectric and topological properties in nanostructured devices.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of precise colloidal nanocrystal synthesis with bulk-sensitive inelastic neutron scattering to probe phonons in quantum dots is unprecedented, allowing direct measurement of confinement effects that are inaccessible to optical techniques or studies of bulk analogues.

proposals.title: Inverse Thermoelectric Design: Maximizing Lattice Thermal Conductivity in Diamondoid and Modulated Chalcogenides for Advanced Heat Spreading
proposals.motivation: High-performance electronics and power modules require materials with exceptionally high lattice thermal conductivity (κ_L) for efficient heat dissipation. While the Kanatzidis group has pioneered strategies to minimize κ_L for thermoelectrics, the inverse problem—systematically maximizing κ_L using similar doping and structural principles—remains underexplored. This work aims to create a new class of high-κ_L semiconductors by applying established defect-engineering knowledge in reverse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying doping and alloying principles, which are known to suppress phonon transport, in a complementary manner—by minimizing point defect scattering and optimizing bond stiffness—can lead to the discovery and synthesis of materials with ultra-high lattice thermal conductivity, particularly within structurally diverse chalcogenide families.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput computational screening using density functional theory (DFT) and the Boltzmann transport equation (BTE) for phonons, as implemented in software like Phonopy and ShengBTE, to calculate κ_L. The search will focus on diamondoid structures (e.g., AgInSe2 derivatives) and incommensurately modulated phases (e.g., A-Ta-Se systems), targeting compositions and dopants that maximize phonon group velocity and minimize anharmonic scattering. Promising candidates will be synthesized via solid-state reactions and characterized using single-crystal X-ray diffraction, with thermal transport properties measured by laser flash analysis and steady-state methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials design paradigm for thermal management, moving beyond traditional high-κ_L materials like diamond and BN. It will yield new synthetic chalcogenides with tailored thermal properties and provide fundamental insights into the structure-property relationships governing phonon transport in complex, modular crystal structures.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit inversion of thermoelectric design rules to maximize, rather than minimize, thermal conductivity within the same families of complex materials, leveraging the group's deep expertise in their synthesis and structural modulation to access uncharted property space.

proposals.title: Systematic Molecular Design of Sulfur and Diammonium Ligands for Enhanced Coordination and Passivation in Hybrid Thermoelectric Materials
proposals.motivation: Effective surface passivation is critical for optimizing the thermoelectric performance of nanostructured and low-dimensional materials by reducing detrimental carrier scattering at interfaces and grain boundaries. Pre-2026 work on hybrid and diamondoid materials, such as AgInSe2, highlights the importance of surface chemistry, but a systematic understanding of how specific molecular structures of passivating agents influence coordination geometry and electronic passivation remains underdeveloped. This knowledge gap limits the rational design of next-generation thermoelectric composites with superior charge transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted variations in the backbone length, functional group substitution, and conformational rigidity of sulfur-containing and diammonium organic molecules will directly tune their coordination strength with inorganic surfaces and their dipole-induced field-effect, leading to predictable enhancements in electronic passivation and carrier mobility.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental synthesis approach. First, density functional theory (DFT) calculations will screen a library of designed organic molecules (e.g., alkanedithiols of varying chain length, aromatic dithiols, and alkyl/aryl diammonium cations) to model their adsorption energetics, coordination modes, and electrostatic potential on model chalcogenide surfaces derived from Kanatzidis-group compounds. Promising candidates will then be synthesized and used in the solution-phase processing of n-type diamondoid analogues (e.g., AgInSe2) and low-dimensional chalcogenides. The resulting materials will be characterized via single-crystal/powder X-ray diffraction to assess structural integration, temperature-dependent Hall effect and Seebeck coefficient measurements to evaluate transport properties, and cross-sectional Kelvin probe force microscopy to quantify surface potential and passivation quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear structure-property relationships between organic ligand architecture and passivation efficacy in hybrid thermoelectrics. It will deliver a set of design rules for molecular passivants, moving beyond trial-and-error. The project is expected to yield new hybrid compositions with significantly reduced thermal conductivity via maintained nanostructuring and enhanced power factor through improved carrier mobility.
proposals.novelty: While molecular surface treatments are common, this proposal uniquely integrates a systematic, chemically diverse ligand design—inspired by the group's expertise in complex chalcogenide structures—with direct quantification of field-effect passivation, a parameter often inferred but rarely measured directly in bulk thermoelectric materials.

proposals.title: Discovery of New Narrow-Gap Chalcogenides with Anharmonic Lattice Dynamics and Multi-Valley Conduction for High Thermoelectric Performance
proposals.motivation: The discovery of ultra-high thermoelectric performance in SnSe, driven by its anharmonic phonon scattering and multi-valley band convergence, established a new paradigm for efficient heat-to-electricity conversion. However, SnSe's performance is highly anisotropic and its constituent elements are relatively scarce, motivating the search for new materials that replicate its advantageous transport mechanisms while offering improved elemental abundance and isotropic properties. This search is critical for advancing thermoelectric technology for waste heat recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted exploration of ternary and quaternary chalcogenides with specific structural motifs (e.g., layered, distorted rock-salt derivatives) and mixed cations will yield new narrow-gap semiconductors exhibiting the intrinsic phonon anharmonicity and complex electronic band structures necessary for high thermoelectric efficiency across both p- and n-type regimes.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental synthesis approach. First, high-throughput density functional theory (DFT) calculations will screen candidate systems (e.g., Ag-Sn-Se, Cu-Sn-S, and related quaternaries with In, Sb, or Bi substitution) for low lattice thermal conductivity (κ_L) via analysis of Gruneisen parameters and for favorable electronic transport via band structure and density-of-states effective mass calculations. Promising candidates will be synthesized as single crystals and polycrystalline pellets using sealed tube reactions and spark plasma sintering. Their structures will be determined by single-crystal X-ray diffraction, and thermoelectric properties (Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, Hall carrier concentration, and thermal conductivity) will be measured from 300 K to 800 K.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new family of high-performance thermoelectric materials beyond SnSe, explicitly linking anharmonic lattice dynamics and band engineering to performance. It will expand the chemical space for efficient thermoelectrics and provide fundamental insights into the design rules for achieving ultralow thermal conductivity and high power factor simultaneously in narrow-gap semiconductors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic targeting of ternary/quaternary phases to decouple and independently optimize the phonon and electron transport mechanisms identified in SnSe, moving beyond simple binary analogs. This approach leverages Prof. Kanatzidis's expertise in complex chalcogenide crystal chemistry and incommensurate structures to discover materials with inherently glass-like thermal transport.

proposals.title: Operando Spectroscopic Mapping of Anharmonic Lattice Dynamics and Electronic Structure Evolution in SnSe Under Thermoelectric Operating Gradients
proposals.motivation: SnSe exhibits record-high ZT values, attributed to its anharmonic, low thermal conductivity lattice and complex electronic band structure. However, its exceptional properties are measured ex-situ, while the material's actual performance is determined under large temperature gradients and electrical currents. A critical knowledge gap exists in understanding how its electronic structure and phonon spectrum dynamically evolve under these real, non-equilibrium operating conditions, limiting rational optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the applied thermal and electrical potentials in operating SnSe induce reversible, gradient-dependent shifts in its valence band maxima and enhance phonon anharmonicity locally, which are the direct microscopic drivers of its ultrahigh thermoelectric efficiency.
proposals.method: We will employ operando Raman spectroscopy and time-resolved X-ray diffraction (using a synchrotron micro-diffraction beamline) on a custom-designed stage that applies simultaneous high temperature gradients (300-800 K) and electrical currents to single-crystal SnSe. This setup allows for spatially resolved mapping of phonon modes and lattice parameters along the gradient. Concurrently, operando high-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on heated stages will track the temperature-dependent evolution of the valence band structure, particularly the convergence of multiple valence bands.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially resolved correlation between operational gradients and the dynamic electronic and vibrational structure of a high-performance thermoelectric. It will establish a foundational operando characterization framework for thermoelectrics, moving beyond averaged properties to identify the local structural motifs responsible for peak performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting operando spectroscopic techniques, common in catalysis, to deconvolve the spatially heterogeneous response of a thermoelectric material's electronic and lattice subsystems under simultaneous thermal and electrical bias, a non-equilibrium state not previously accessible.

proposals.title: Engineering Topological Band Inversions in SnSe via Alloying and Strain for Enhanced Thermoelectric Transport
proposals.motivation: SnSe is a preeminent thermoelectric material due to its ultralow thermal conductivity, but its electronic performance, particularly for n-type conduction, is limited by its band structure. Topological insulators (TIs), such as Bi2Te3, possess protected metallic surface states with high carrier mobility and spin-momentum locking, which could dramatically enhance electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient if integrated into a high-performance thermoelectric matrix. Transferring TI design principles to SnSe could unlock a new paradigm for thermoelectrics.
proposals.hypothesis: By introducing specific isovalent or aliovalent alloying (e.g., with Pb, Ge, or Sb) and applying controlled biaxial strain, we can engineer a band inversion in SnSe's electronic structure, creating topologically non-trivial surface states while preserving its advantageous low lattice thermal conductivity.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput first-principles DFT calculations (VASP, using HSE06 hybrid functionals) to screen for alloying elements and strain conditions that induce a band inversion at the Γ or Z point of the Brillouin zone in the Pnma phase. Promising compositions will be synthesized via solid-state reaction and Bridgman crystal growth to obtain high-quality single crystals for definitive structural (single-crystal XRD) and electronic characterization. Transport properties (electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, Hall effect) will be measured on oriented single crystals to probe the emergence of surface-dominated conduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials design strategy for thermoelectrics by merging topological insulator physics with an intrinsically low-thermal-conductivity host. It will yield the first demonstration of a topological SnSe derivative, providing fundamental insight into the interplay between topology and thermoelectric performance and potentially discovering a new high-zT n-type variant.
proposals.novelty: While topological insulators and SnSe have been studied separately, the deliberate engineering of topological states within the SnSe lattice via combined chemical and strain tuning is a novel and unexplored approach to transcend its conventional electronic transport limits.

proposals.title: Targeted Phonon Engineering: Designing High-Temperature Thermal Barrier Coatings via Structural Principles from SnSe
proposals.motivation: High-performance thermal barrier coatings (TBCs) are critical for improving the efficiency and durability of gas turbines and aerospace engines. Current state-of-the-art yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) faces limitations above 1200°C, necessitating new materials with intrinsically low and stable lattice thermal conductivity (κ_lat). The discovery of ultra-low κ_lat in SnSe, arising from its anharmonic, layered structure, provides a foundational design principle for such coatings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by deconstructing the phonon scattering mechanisms in SnSe—specifically, its strong anharmonicity, soft bonding, and structural complexity—we can rationally design and synthesize new, oxidation-resistant chalcogenides and pnictides with similarly suppressed κ_lat, optimized for high-temperature coating applications.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental approach. First, density functional theory (DFT) and anharmonic lattice dynamics calculations will screen candidate systems (e.g., layered selenides, tellurides, and modulated structures akin to A14+2/3Ta8Se46+2/3) to predict their phonon spectra and κ_lat. Guided by these predictions, we will synthesize target compounds using single-crystal and polycrystalline methods established in the Kanatzidis group. Their thermal transport properties will be characterized via laser flash analysis, differential scanning calorimetry, and in-situ high-temperature X-ray diffraction to validate design principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct materials design pathway from fundamental thermoelectric insights (phonon scattering) to applied thermal management. It will yield new candidate materials with quantified thermal insulation performance and elucidate the role of specific structural motifs (e.g., off-centering, incommensurate modulations) in achieving ultralow κ_lat in coating-relevant compounds.
proposals.novelty: While SnSe's properties are studied for thermoelectrics, this proposal inverts the problem to deliberately engineer phonon conductors for TBCs, a distinct application space. It uniquely leverages the group's expertise in complex, modulated chalcogenide structures—beyond simple SnSe analogues—to discover new coating materials.

ideas.title: Dynamic Lone Pair Engineering via Chemical Pressure from Anionic Alloying
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to systematically dope the Te site in SnTe-based or related Sn2+-containing chalcogenides with larger or smaller anions (e.g., Se, S) to apply chemical pressure. Crucially, the chemical pressure is not used merely to optimize a static property, but to dynamically manipulate the expression and stereochemical activity of the Sn2+ 5s2 lone pair. By creating compositionally graded samples or solid-solution series, we can directly correlate the degree of lone pair distortion—its 'dynamic tunability'—with changes in local structure, electronic band structure, and emergent properties like piezoelectric or nonlinear optical responses, which are inaccessible by considering site doping or lone pair effects in isolation.

ideas.title: Systematic Screening of Isoelectronic and Aliovalent Small-Radius Dopants for SnTe via a High-Throughput Computational Framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of high-throughput computational screening from the field of catalysis and materials discovery for hydrogen storage. In that analogous field, researchers efficiently navigate vast chemical spaces by systematically calculating key properties of potential dopants or alloying elements to identify optimal candidates for experimental testing. The adapted research idea applies this methodology to the original problem of finding other small-radius dopants for chemical pressure in SnTe. We propose defining a search space of potential isoelectronic and aliovalent dopants based on strict atomic/ionic radius criteria and valence states. Using a high-throughput DFT framework, we would rapidly screen these candidates by predicting their incorporation energy, induced lattice strain, and, crucially, their effect on band convergence (specifically the valence band offset between L and Σ points) and thermoelectric power factor. This computational pre-screening would generate a prioritized shortlist of the most theoretically promising dopants (e.g., specific transition metals or other elements not previously considered), thereby guiding and accelerating the subsequent experimental synthesis and characterization work to develop higher-efficiency SnTe-based thermoelectrics.

ideas.title: Investigate lone pair dynamics via in situ spectroscopic mapping under thermodynamic perturbation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from protein conformational dynamics, where in situ spectroscopic and scattering techniques under variable temperature/pressure map structural energy landscapes. The analogous problem in FASnI3-xBrx is understanding how the dynamic expression of the Sn2+ 5s2 lone pair, a key electronic structural feature, is modulated by thermodynamic conditions. The adapted solution is to employ in situ variable-temperature and variable-pressure X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and pair distribution function (PDF) analysis. This approach will directly probe the local coordination geometry and electron density around the Sn2+ cation, allowing the construction of a dynamic map that correlates lone pair stereochemical activity with antiferroelectric lattice distortions across a defined temperature-pressure phase diagram.

ideas.title: Challenge necessity of high crystal symmetry
ideas.description: Explore if deliberately low-symmetry or amorphous phases can provide superior decoupling of electron and phonon transport.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategies and Prospects for High-Performance Te-Free Thermoelectric Materials.

ideas.title: Decoupling Electronic and Structural Pressure via Topological Dopant Design
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that chemical pressure necessarily shrinks the lattice by seeking to decouple electronic and structural effects. Drawing an analogy from protein engineering and allosteric regulation, where modulators bind remotely to induce functional changes without direct steric blockage, we propose a strategy for dopant design in solid-state materials. The analogous solution involves designing allosteric modulators that transmit signals through specific structural pathways. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to design 'topological' or 'allosteric' dopants that exert electronic pressure primarily through orbital interactions—such as symmetry breaking, band filling, or Fermi surface distortion—rather than through ionic radius mismatch. This would involve computational screening and experimental synthesis of materials where dopants are selected for their electronic structure (e.g., d-electron count, orbital symmetry, electronegativity) at lattice sites that are electronically coupled but structurally buffered from the key bonds determining lattice parameters. The goal is to create a scenario of 'pure' electronic pressure, enabling the study of electronic correlations, superconductivity, or magnetic transitions independent of confounding lattice contraction.

ideas.title: Develop new materials with SnSe-like characteristics
ideas.description: Discover and engineer other narrow-gap semiconductors using transport mechanisms identified in SnSe research.
ideas.sourceTitle: The development and impact of tin selenide on thermoelectrics

ideas.title: Model electronic structure with advanced DFT
ideas.description: Perform DFT calculations incorporating lone pair effects and disorder to model band structure evolution and trap states across the series.
ideas.sourceTitle: Structural Evolution and Photoluminescence Quenching across the FASnI3-xBrx (x = 0-3) Perovskites.

ideas.title: Invert the high carrier mobility priority
ideas.description: Investigate materials where low electron mobility is compensated by extremely low thermal conductivity for high ZT.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategies and Prospects for High-Performance Te-Free Thermoelectric Materials.

ideas.title: Local Structural 'Allostery' as the Primary Determinant of Optoelectronic Properties
ideas.description: This idea inverts the focus from average to local structure as the primary determinant of optoelectronic properties like photoluminescence (PL). The analogy is drawn from structural biology, where the shift from viewing proteins as static, average crystals to dynamic ensembles of local conformations revolutionized the understanding of function (e.g., allostery, enzyme catalysis). The adapted research idea proposes to treat functional materials similarly: the critical optoelectronic properties are not defined by the average crystallographic structure but by the populations and dynamics of local structural states (e.g., specific lattice distortions, ion arrangements, or defect complexes). By adapting high-resolution, local-probe spectroscopic and imaging techniques from biophysics—and developing new ones for materials science—the goal is to map these local structural ensembles and establish direct, causative links between specific local structural motifs and discrete material performance metrics, moving beyond correlative studies based on averaged measurements.

ideas.title: Probe the role of Sn/Ge compositional heterogeneity in the structural and thermal degradation of mixed Sn-Ge perovskites
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the assumption of Pb-Sn structural analogy by using the formation and evolution of Ge-rich nanoprecipitates in mixed Sn-Ge perovskites as a structural probe. It investigates how the distinct lattice dynamics and bonding preferences of tin, especially under halide variation, drive the nucleation, growth, and dissolution of these Ge-rich phases during thermal and operational aging. The goal is to establish a causal relationship between the perovskite's inherent (non-lead-like) structural instability and the temporal evolution of its heterogeneous microstructure, providing a fundamental mechanism for degradation in this lead-free material class.

ideas.title: Optimize ligand structure for hole-transport layer interface
ideas.description: Design dual-site ligands that also minimize energy mismatch with hole-transport materials.
ideas.sourceTitle: Improved charge extraction in inverted perovskite solar cells with dual-site-binding ligands

ideas.title: Assume Pb and Sn perovskites behave identically
ideas.description: Relax the assumption that lead and tin perovskites are structurally analogous under halide substitution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Structural Evolution and Photoluminescence Quenching across the FASnI3-xBrx (x = 0-3) Perovskites.

ideas.title: Mapping the Chemical Pressure-Driven Electronic Phase Diagram of SnTe via Te-Site Doping
ideas.description: This research direction combines heavy Te-site doping/alloying in SnTe (to apply chemical pressure) with a deliberate search for non-monotonic electronic structure changes. The core hypothesis is that chemical pressure from Te-site substitution may drive the system through subtle structural or electronic phase transitions that cause discontinuities in bandgap evolution, carrier effective mass, or topological character. By performing high-resolution doping studies combined with detailed transport, spectroscopic, and computational analysis, the work aims to construct a detailed electronic phase diagram, identifying critical doping thresholds where electronic properties change abruptly rather than smoothly.

ideas.title: Direct measurement of quantum-confined phonon softening in SnTe nanocrystals via inelastic neutron scattering
ideas.description: This research direction combines the synthesis of size-tunable SnTe quantum dots with high-resolution inelastic neutron scattering to directly probe how quantum confinement and the resulting internal chemical pressure modify phonon dispersion relations. By systematically varying nanocrystal size and surface chemistry, the experiment will isolate the contribution of 'discordant' surface atoms and dimensional confinement to the softening of transverse optical phonon modes, which is linked to the enhancement of thermoelectric and topological properties. This approach uniquely allows for the quantification of phonon anharmonicity and mode-specific Grüneisen parameters under nanoscale pressure, providing fundamental insights not accessible in bulk or thin-film studies. The results will establish design principles for engineering phonon spectra in quantum-confined topological materials for advanced thin-film devices.

ideas.title: Challenge temperature stability as fixed property
ideas.description: Develop materials whose electronic or structural properties adaptively change with temperature to optimize performance across a range.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategies and Prospects for High-Performance Te-Free Thermoelectric Materials.

ideas.title: Prioritize Topological Band Engineering Over Simple Proximity
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology, where major innovations arise from genetic network rewiring rather than simple trait convergence, this idea challenges the assumption that band convergence is best achieved through incremental tuning. Instead, it proposes prioritizing research into mechanisms that cause extreme band distortion or the emergence of entirely new bands over efforts focused solely on reducing the L-Σ energy gap. The adapted solution is to identify and manipulate the fundamental 'toolkit' interactions in the electronic structure—such as specific spin-orbit coupling terms, orbital hybridization patterns, or symmetry-breaking lattice distortions—that can radically reshape the band topology. The primary goal shifts from achieving simple energetic proximity to engineering a qualitatively new band configuration that enables superior thermoelectric or electronic performance.

ideas.title: Transfer topological insulator design principles to SnSe
ideas.description: Engineer SnSe's band structure to introduce topological surface states for enhanced electronic transport.
ideas.sourceTitle: The development and impact of tin selenide on thermoelectrics

ideas.title: Systematic detection of structural and electronic phase transitions governing non-monotonic property evolution
ideas.description: This project directly targets the root cause of non-monotonic property changes by developing a computational framework to simultaneously track structural stability and the topology of electronic states across a material phase space. It uses automated workflows to identify not only structural phase boundaries but also electronic topological transitions (Lifshitz transitions) and changes in band inversion, which can cause discontinuous changes in properties without a change in crystal symmetry. The output is a comprehensive map linking composition and strain to all types of electronic structure discontinuities, providing a predictive model for anomalous property evolution in alloy systems and heterostructures.

ideas.title: Competing Epitaxial Strain and Isovalent Doping to Decouple Lattice and Electronic Pressure
ideas.description: This redesigned approach decouples lattice and electronic effects by applying two independent, competing tuning knobs to a thin-film system. Epitaxial strain from a graded substrate library directly controls the lattice constant externally, while isovalent doping alters the electronic pressure via changes in orbital hybridization and band structure without introducing charge carriers or significant ionic size mismatch. This platform allows for mapping a phase diagram where the effects of physical lattice strain and chemical electronic pressure can be varied independently and in opposition, revealing their true individual contributions to material properties. The use of thin-film combinatorial libraries enables high-throughput screening of this decoupled parameter space.

ideas.title: Detect Hidden Phase Transitions via High-Resolution Spectroscopic Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the common assumption that bandgap tuning in materials (e.g., via alloying, strain, or dimensionality change) is a smooth, monotonic process. Drawing an analogy from biochemistry's protein folding problem—where the simple two-state folding model was upended by the discovery of complex intermediates and pathways—we propose to adapt its solution strategy. In protein folding, high-resolution, multi-technique spectroscopic fingerprinting (e.g., HDX-MS) revealed non-monotonic energy landscapes and hidden states. Adapted to electronic materials, the research will employ a coordinated suite of high-resolution, in-situ spectroscopic and diffraction probes during the bandgap tuning process. By looking for correlated, abrupt changes in multiple spectroscopic signatures (electronic, vibrational, structural), the aim is to detect and characterize any discontinuous electronic structure evolution or hidden phase transitions that violate the smooth monotonicity assumption, fundamentally refining our understanding of structure-property relationships in tunable semiconductors and heterostructures.

ideas.title: Study nanocrystals to confine and control distortions
ideas.description: Scale down to nanocrystals where surface effects and quantum confinement may alter lone pair expression and PL quenching.
ideas.sourceTitle: Structural Evolution and Photoluminescence Quenching across the FASnI3-xBrx (x = 0-3) Perovskites.

ideas.title: Correlating 3D Atomic-Scale Chemistry with Local Phonon Softening in Nanostructured Alloys
ideas.description: This research direction combines atom probe tomography (APT) and inelastic neutron scattering (INS) to establish a direct structure-property relationship at the atomic scale in nanostructured alloys. APT provides the precise 3D chemical identity and position of every atom within a volume containing Ge-rich nanoprecipitates and their surrounding matrix, mapping solute segregation and interfacial chemistry. The same specimen, or specimens from the same controlled material batch, is then studied with INS to measure the full phonon dispersion, identifying softened modes. The synergy lies in using the atomically precise chemical map from APT to inform lattice dynamical models, allowing the INS data to be interpreted not as a bulk average but as a superposition of distinct vibrational signatures originating from the specific local chemical environments identified by APT. This creates a closed loop where chemical heterogeneity is no longer a confounding variable but the explicit input for understanding phonon-mediated properties like thermal conductivity and stability.

ideas.title: Test other transition metal dopants in 2D perovskites
ideas.description: Apply the same methodology to Co2+ and Ni2+ dopants to determine if they also occupy interstitial sites.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interstitial Nature of Mn2+ Doping in 2D Perovskites.

ideas.title: Ultrafast Spectroscopy Guided by First-Principles Dynamics for Non-Radiative Pathway Engineering
ideas.description: This research direction integrates advanced DFT calculations, specifically modeling the dynamic evolution of lone pair-induced distortions and structural disorder, with ultrafast spectroscopic probes of the same transient local structure. The synergy lies in using the ultrafast experimental data as a direct, time-resolved benchmark for the DFT-predicted potential energy surfaces and electron-phonon coupling strengths associated with non-radiative traps. This feedback loop allows for the iterative refinement of theoretical models to accurately simulate the real-time atomic motion leading to PL quenching. Consequently, the validated dynamic models can then be used to computationally screen and design new compositions or heterostructures where these detrimental distortion pathways are inherently suppressed.

ideas.title: Optimize molecular structures for enhanced passivation
ideas.description: Systematically vary the chemical structures of the sulfur and diammonium molecules to further improve coordination and field-effect passivation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bimolecularly passivated interface enables efficient and stable inverted perovskite solar cells

ideas.title: Chemical pressure must shrink the lattice
ideas.description: Explore dopants that create electronic pressure without lattice shrinkage to decouple effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: Chemical Pressure-Driven Band Convergence and Discordant Atoms Intensify Phonon Scattering Leading to High Thermoelectric Performance in SnTe.

ideas.title: Sn site doping is the only effective path
ideas.description: Investigate heavy doping or alloying on the Te site to create chemical pressure.
ideas.sourceTitle: Chemical Pressure-Driven Band Convergence and Discordant Atoms Intensify Phonon Scattering Leading to High Thermoelectric Performance in SnTe.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Design phonon conductors using SnSe insights
ideas.description: Apply knowledge of SnSe's low lattice thermal conductivity to engineer materials for thermal barrier coatings.
ideas.sourceTitle: The development and impact of tin selenide on thermoelectrics

ideas.title: Correlate local structural distortions under chemical pressure with emergent mechanical and optoelectronic properties
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how chemical pressure, applied via doping or stoichiometric variation, creates local lattice distortions and strain fields that deviate from the average crystal structure. It seeks to determine how these specific local structural motifs simultaneously govern fracture toughness (a bulk mechanical property) and localized optoelectronic behaviors like photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield or spectral shifts. By employing spatially resolved techniques such as scanning probe microscopy and nanoindentation coupled with local spectroscopy, the work will establish causal relationships between chemically induced local structure and multifunctional property evolution. This moves beyond studying averages to engineer materials where targeted local environments are designed to optimize both mechanical resilience and electronic performance.

ideas.title: Multimodal ultrafast spectroscopy and element-specific structural dynamics to atomistically resolve non-radiative pathways
ideas.description: This approach integrates ultrafast optical spectroscopy with simultaneous, element-specific time-resolved structural probes, such as X-ray absorption fine structure (tr-XAFS) or electron diffraction, performed at a femtosecond-pulsed source. The combined measurement directly links the electronic relaxation dynamics causing photoluminescence quenching to the precise, sub-Ångstrom evolution of local bond lengths and coordination geometries around specific atomic sites. This enables the unambiguous identification of which transient local distortions—such as specific bond stretching, coordination loss, or symmetry breaking—act as the dominant non-radiative recombination pathway. The result is a causally established, atomistic mechanism for efficiency loss, rather than a temporal correlation.

ideas.title: Mapping Chemical Pressure-Induced Phonon Softening in Complex SnTe Alloys via Resonant Ultrasound Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This research direction employs resonant ultrasound spectroscopy (RUS) to directly measure and quantify phonon mode softening in complex, chemically pressurized SnTe-based ternary and quaternary alloy systems. By systematically varying alloy composition (e.g., SnTe-MnTe-PbTe or SnTe-SrTe-NaSbTe2), RUS provides a sensitive, non-destructive probe of how specific defect configurations and local strain fields from chemical pressure alter the elastic tensor and resonant frequencies. This allows for the creation of a detailed map linking compositional complexity to specific phonon softening mechanisms, moving beyond bulk property measurements. The synergy enables the rational design of next-generation thermoelectric materials by identifying the precise alloy combinations that optimize phonon scattering for minimum lattice thermal conductivity without compromising electronic transport.

ideas.title: Hierarchical ab initio modeling of electronic structure and traps with GW and embedded wavefunction corrections
ideas.description: This project employs a tiered computational strategy to accurately model band structure evolution and defect states in disordered materials with lone pairs. The workflow begins with advanced DFT calculations on large supercells to model disorder and identify candidate trap configurations. Crucially, the quasiparticle band structure and shallow trap levels are then computed using the GW method to correct the DFT band gap and level positions. For deep, strongly correlated trap states, embedded cluster models treated with high-level multireference wavefunction methods provide definitive characterization. This integrated approach overcomes the fundamental accuracy limits of standalone DFT, delivering quantitatively reliable predictions for comparison with experiment.

ideas.title: Assume indirect gap limits optoelectronic performance
ideas.description: Engineer the band structure to create a direct gap variant of SnP2Se6 to potentially enhance light absorption and emission.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bulk photovoltaic effect and high mobility in the polar 2D semiconductor SnP2Se6

ideas.title: Dual problem: use BPVE current to probe crystal symmetry
ideas.description: Use the magnitude and direction of the bulk photocurrent as a sensitive probe of local crystal symmetry and defects.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bulk photovoltaic effect and high mobility in the polar 2D semiconductor SnP2Se6

ideas.title: Co-optimization of CdS Alloying Fraction and Synthesis-Processing Pathways for Robust Performance
ideas.description: This research systematically explores the interdependent effects of CdS alloying percentage, synthesis method, and processing conditions on thermoelectric performance. Instead of varying composition alone, a design-of-experiments approach is used to fabricate samples across a matrix of alloy fractions, synthesis routes, and annealing protocols. Each sample is characterized for its microstructural state (phase purity, grain size, defect density) alongside measurements of band structure (via UV-Vis and XPS) and phonon scattering (via thermal conductivity and Raman spectroscopy). The resulting multi-parameter dataset identifies the specific combination of composition and processing required to achieve the optimal balance between band convergence and phonon scattering, ensuring findings are robust and transferable to practical fabrication.

ideas.title: Deconvolving Local Mechanisms of Phonon Softening via Multi-Modal, Element-Specific Lattice Dynamics
ideas.description: This research employs a tightly integrated, multi-modal scattering approach to isolate the distinct mechanisms by which discordant atoms induce phonon softening. The core methodology combines inelastic neutron scattering for the full phonon dispersion with element-specific resonant inelastic X-ray scattering and local structural probes like XAFS. This combination allows for the direct correlation of specific local atomic distortions and electronic states with changes in the collective lattice dynamics. The resulting dataset is analyzed through a machine-learning-assisted modeling framework to quantitatively assign observed softening to specific mechanisms such as local strain, charge redistribution, or dynamic short-range order.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Maximize thermal conductivity
ideas.description: Use the same doping principles to create high lattice thermal conductivity materials for heat spreaders.
ideas.sourceTitle: Chemical Pressure-Driven Band Convergence and Discordant Atoms Intensify Phonon Scattering Leading to High Thermoelectric Performance in SnTe.

ideas.title: Scale down to single dopant atom studies
ideas.description: Investigate the properties of a single interstitial Mn2+ ion using single-spin resonance techniques.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interstitial Nature of Mn2+ Doping in 2D Perovskites.

ideas.title: Integrate synthesized crystals into functional optoelectronic devices
ideas.description: Fabricate and test devices like LEDs or photodetectors using the phase-pure, high-n crystals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Synthesis of 2D perovskite crystals via progressive transformation of quantum well thickness

ideas.title: Investigate lone pair dynamics with temperature or pressure
ideas.description: Study how temperature or pressure modulates the dynamic 5s2 lone pair expression and associated antiferroelectric distortions in FASnI3-xBrx.
ideas.sourceTitle: Structural Evolution and Photoluminescence Quenching across the FASnI3-xBrx (x = 0-3) Perovskites.

ideas.title: Apply phase-change material concepts
ideas.description: Integrate materials with reversible structural phase transitions to dynamically tune thermal conductivity with temperature.
ideas.sourceTitle: Strategies and Prospects for High-Performance Te-Free Thermoelectric Materials.

ideas.title: Phonon Anti-Resonances: Using Discordant Atoms to Constructively Interfere Phonon Modes
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that discordant atom combinations always reduce thermal conductivity by drawing an analogy from optics. In photonics, multi-layer thin films use materials with discordant refractive indices not just for destructive interference (anti-reflection) but for constructive interference to enhance light transmission. The analogous solution is to deliberately engineer the placement of 'discordant' atomic layers within a material's structure—like in a superlattice—to act as phononic interference coatings. By carefully controlling the spacing and composition of these layers relative to dominant phonon wavelengths, the research would test if constructive interference for specific phonon modes can be achieved, potentially creating a passband that increases thermal conductivity in a targeted frequency regime, turning a typical scattering mechanism into a transport-enhancing one.

ideas.title: High-throughput screening of dopants under programmable epitaxial strain
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-throughput DFT screening with programmable epitaxial strain to create a novel computational-experimental feedback loop. The computational screening is performed not just for dopants in a static lattice, but across a continuous range of simulated biaxial strain states to identify optimal dopant-strain pairs for target properties like conductivity or catalytic activity. These predicted optimal combinations then guide the epitaxial growth of thin films where the identified strain is applied as a tunable, uniform chemical pressure. This synergy allows for the systematic exploration and realization of materials states that are inaccessible through doping or strain alone, accelerating the discovery of optimized functional materials.

ideas.title: Epitaxial Strain Engineering of SnTe Quantum Dot Superlattices for Topological Device Tuning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes the synthesis of highly ordered, epitaxial superlattices of SnTe quantum dots on lattice-mismatched substrates (e.g., CdTe, PbSe) to impose a controlled, biaxial strain field across the entire nanocrystal array. The epitaxial strain provides a uniform chemical pressure that tunes the topological crystalline insulator phase and band inversion strength in each quantum dot coherently, while quantum confinement from the dot size independently modulates the electronic structure. This dual-parameter control (strain + confinement) within an epitaxial architecture allows for the precise engineering of Dirac cone velocity and surface state spatial overlap in a scalable thin-film format, enabling novel device concepts in low-power electronics and quantum information based on tailored topological interfaces.

ideas.title: Relax requirement for phase-pure single crystals
ideas.description: Study the optoelectronic utility of intentionally engineered gradient n-value structures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Synthesis of 2D perovskite crystals via progressive transformation of quantum well thickness

ideas.title: Scale down to monolayer to probe quantum confinement
ideas.description: Isolate a single monolayer of SnP2Se6 to study how quantum confinement affects its bandgap and photovoltaic properties.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bulk photovoltaic effect and high mobility in the polar 2D semiconductor SnP2Se6

ideas.title: Screen for dual-site ligands for other defects
ideas.description: Discover ligands that bind to other defect pairs, like lead-iodide or organic cation vacancies.
